Dear Case,

I had something in mind, in order to shift large buffers: today, I do this
the ugly way, see
https://github.com/mitshell/libmich/blob/master/libmich/core/shtr.py#L157.
This helps me to process bitstream, like it is required with ASN.1 PER:
fields are encoded / decoded bit by bit, in the minimum number of bits. I
understand that the handling of most significant zero bits within mpz is
limiting here, so I guess it won't easily help me for this case.
I have another corner case, still with ASN.1, where it is possible to
encode / decode unbounded integers (e.g. over 64kb, ...). I am not aware of
any real-life protocol that would make use of such value, but the standard
and encoding / decoding rules allow it ! Python starts to show its limit
with such big integers, so using gmpy2 would help here. However, most
significant bytes are leftmost with ASN.1, this would be a reason to get
access at least to the "order" parameter, or even better to the raw
mpz_import() / export() functions.

Best Regards
Benoit

The rationale for the debouncing is that if a task reads once, there's a pretty good chance it's going to read again.   For example, reading some headers on a request and then reading a request body.  If a task writes, there's a good chance it's going to write again.  For example, writing a large response in chunks.  I consider both of these cases to be real-world scenarios--not just something from an echo server. 

I consider any situation where two tasks are blocked on the same descriptor to be an error.  It's already an error in the selectors module so far as I can tell.  Real screwy things happen if you do things like this with threads as well (at least in my experience).     If someone really wants multiple tasks to be doing something with the same descriptor, they should probably use os.dup() on it and work with that.  

The case of closing/reopening should be looked at though.  That is a bug.  

As a side-note: some other async frameworks seem to make a similar "optimization" by simply leaving the file descriptor attached to the event loop and performing their own internal buffering.  This ties it to the buffer-bloat problem.  There is peril everywhere.

Can you from Quantity get value (`1.0`), prefix (`kilo`) and unit (`meter`) ?

Yes. This is long desired feature (See #15).  Following your code, we need first to define a sorting order for the output of `compatible_units` and we do not have yet a good way to do it. (That is why we return a frozenset and not a tuple). See the discussions in #89 

But it gets more complex. For example, certain compound units (eg. km**2 / degK) cannot be represented in a single unit. So we need to do a combinatorial scan of how it can be written in terms of existing units. I have some code in my private repo that used OrthogonalMatchingPursuit to find the shortest combination of units (base and derived) with the same dimensionality of the input units. It was working for some cases, but not giving good results in others.

I am +1 on this if it can be achieved. Please look at the issues that I pointed for previous discussions and also at #151

Update on this - better error handling code for redis urls is going in to the next release, which I hope to have out today.

On your 1) and 2), I don't actually have a better solution.

Hi Dominique,

this is a good question and I wish I could give you a good answer. I implemented these algorithms in order to understand them myself. The correct choice of parameters still remains somewhat of a mystery to me and I think an arbitrary choice is a potential weakness of any analysis performed with these measures since you can often find a parameter set that shows what you want to show but is not necessarily a real effect in your data.

I actually use Nolds for the analysis of heart-rate variability data (series of RR-intervals) and I set the correlation dimention to 2 for both `sampen` and `corr_dim`, but this is totally arbitrary. I will have to come up with a plausible heuristic myself.

I would suggest that you turn to the literature and try to find other people that used the same measures with the same kind of data and hopefully report parameter settings (that hopefully are not also arbitrary :wink:). Another approach would be to use a set of sample data where you know that e.g. the Sample Entropy should be higher for dataset A than for dataset B. If you have enough data with preexisting diagnoses, you can try different parameter settings and select the one that gives the most plausible results on your sample data.

I will close this issue, since it is not an issue with the code but a question regarding the algorithms. If you want, you can contact me by email. You can find my [contatc information on my website](https://homepages.thm.de/~cslz90/).

Regards,
Christopher

@arose the frame count can be gotten from `Universe.trajectory.n_frames` or equivalently `len(Universe.trajectory)`. Getting the topology as a string is possible using [`MDAnalysis.lib.util.NamedStream`](http://pythonhosted.org/MDAnalysis/documentation_pages/lib/util.html?highlight=namedstream#MDAnalysis.lib.util.NamedStream), perhaps like so:

```python
import MDAnalysis as mda
from MDAnalysisTests.datafiles import GRO, XTC
import cStringIO

u = mda.Universe(GRO, XTC)

f = mda.lib.util.NamedStream(cStringIO.StringIO(), 'lol.pdb')

# add PDB output to the named stream
with mda.Writer(f, u.atoms.n_atoms, multiframe=False) as W:
    W.write(u.atoms)

# extract from the stream
f.readlines()
```

The short answer is no.

I suggest two alternatives:

    (1)  If you just need to cache a single value (the result of
longcomputation() from your example) then forget Pylru.  Just cache
the single value inside the object yourself.  Then you can implement
the logic to recompute the value appropriately inside of
longcomputation() and update().

    (2)  If (as I assume) your actual usage is more involved than the
example and you need an LRU cache to cache multiple values, then use
Pylru but forget the lrudecorator().  Setup an LRU cache object inside
of your object at initialization.  Manage it with some simple logic
inside of longcomputation() and update().

Hope this helps.

I've stumbled also to another issue. My shortcut's path has a non-ascii letter in it:

shortcut.lnk --> C:\Tiedostot\Henkilökohtainen (Finnish language, C:\Files\Personal in English)

If I do this:
```python
print u"öäå", winshell.shortcut(".\\shortcut.lnk").path
```
I get output:
```öäå C:\Tiedostot\Henkil÷kohtainen```

Then, by trial and error, I found that with cp1252 encoding I can get the path out the way it should be:
```python
print u"öäå", unicode(winshell.shortcut(".\\shortcut.lnk").path, encoding="cp1252")
```
Output:
```öäå C:\Tiedostot\Henkilökohtainen```

However, hard coding the the cp1252 encoding might not be smart, in case some other letters are used which are not included in the encoding.

As an alternative solution, I found out that win32com returns the path as 'unicode' type, and prints out correctly:
```python
from win32com.client import Dispatch
print u"öäå", Dispatch("WScript.Shell").CreateShortCut(".\\shortcut.lnk").TargetPath
```
Output:
```öäå C:\Tiedostot\Henkilökohtainen```

Not really. `graphviz.Source` just represents DOT source as a string, i.e. this libary does not parse (only generates) DOT source code.

If your exported DOT does not really contain style/formatting directives, you might be able to manually insert `attr()` statements such as `node [shape=rarrow]` before your node and edge statements right after the first `{` in your string (or automatically with string replacement or `re`).

If you want to do more complex transformations to your DOT code (e.g. 'do X with all node statements that...'), you might consider a library that (also) parses it such as [pygraphviz](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pygraphviz) or [pydot](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pydot).

The `do` method declaration for the `Job` class is
```python
def do(job_func, *args, **kwargs)
```
The docs say that `args` and `kwargs` are passed on to the `job_func` so an example would be:
```python
import schedule, time

def func_takes_arg(arg):
    print(arg)

schedule.every(2).seconds.do(func_takes_arg, "Hello")
schedule.every(2).seconds.do(func_takes_arg, arg="World")

while True:
    schedule.run_pending()
    time.sleep(1)
```
The above code should print "Hello" and "World" every 2 seconds.

Remap keys in my opinion will have edge case problems depending on the object structure, also I think we can take that discussion to a new enhancement issue. So instead what I think would be useful method be not exactly remap keys but more of a combination of `py_.get` and `py_.pick`. Something like:
```python
def method(obj, key_map):
    new_obj = dict()
    for key, value in key_map:
        set(new_obj, value, get(obj, key)
    return new_obj
```
so usage example would look like:

@laurentlgm Adding some notes to this discussion mostly for future reference.

For this pattern you should use the `generic_termserver_telnet` and then manually handle the telnet login (and then redispatch). 

I made this change to the `generic_termserver` so that by default the terminal server telnet driver does NOT log you into the device:

https://github.com/ktbyers/netmiko/commit/2b273764fe5cf262571049c03c361201ed9c9c11 search for terminal_server.

After that change is made, you will need to either manually call `net_connect.std_login()` (where net_connect is the Netmiko connection object) or manually handle the telnet login. In both cases you should `redispatch` after done.

I will probably integrate your PR in for the default telnet login (PR #524) in. It is somewhat of an accidental artifact that this code actually logs you in. In other words, it is not intentionally designed to handle multiple levels of authentication.

> An `--exit-on-skip` option that just exits the program on any download-skip would certainly be possible.

Yes, for example. I think the current behaviour is just right as the default, we'll see when someone asks for other variants.

The features to achieve what you described already exist. Try out the `--abort-on-skip` command-line option or, by extension, the [`skip` option](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractorskip) if you are using a config file.

By default all downloads of files which have already been downloaded (i.e. the file exists in the filesystem) are skipped. `--abort-on-skip` sets the [`skip` option](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractorskip) to `"abort"` and tells gallery-dl to stop working on the current gallery URL and proceed to the next one instead of just skipping a download.

Initially, `ipdb.routes['default']` returned a reference to a route, not a list. So I believe there may be a code which depends on it.

So if we change the result to list — it is an API change. Backwards-incompatible, and since the library is in RHEL updates, such a change would be a bad idea.

If we do so it returns a reference if there is one default route, and a list when there are several default routes — it is a mess in types.

So I decided yet to provide a list-like API to routing tables, so one can iterate, filter out default routes and use them. Not an optimal solution yet.

When I forwarded this bug report, I hadn't realised that it was the redirection that was causing the autoreset codes to be omitted. Thanks for catching that @wiggin15!

The aim behind the fix for issue 18 was that Colorama should behave like 'ls' or 'grep', which only put ANSI color codes in their output if it is going to a terminal. If their stdout is redirected to a file, they do not use color (unless you add extra flags to force them to do so.)

If that is deemed to be desirable behaviour, then:

(a) the omission of the autoreset codes is working as intended, and
(b) perhaps some work needs to be done to make this behaviour more consistent (e.g. perhaps the initial '←[31m' codes should be stripped as well?)

On the other hand, it does seem arguable that maybe this would be better handled at the application level, and colorama should simply always print color codes when it is asked to. The downside of that though, is that lots of diverse applications would have to replicate these 'is_tty' checks, and of course it's a large change in compatibility, which I'd be very nervous about given Colorama's surprisingly large install base.

Thanks a lot for the example app, it's great! The problem seems to be caused by having ``ModelSerializer``s with [implicitly generated ``NestedSerializer``](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/d2994e0596c3163ac970b29dad6a61485f938045/rest_framework/serializers.py#L1240)s for their model's ``ForeignKey`` relations...

It can be worked around by defining nested fields explicitly on your ``ModelSerializer`` to indicate which serializer they should use. 

The permanent fix will likely be to force all such automatic serializers to be output inline (e.g. no entry in the bottom ``Models`` section, no sub-model name when viewing the operation). If you want to keep the named/by-reference behaviour you will have to implement the solution mentioned above anyway.

This is likely an artifact of the group coordination protocol. If the group already exists, the coordinator will keep open the JoinRequest until all known members check-in. In this case, notice that your group generation is 4, which means that the group has already been through several rounds already.

So two suggestions: (1) make sure you run `consumer.close()` if you are intentionally killing a consumer but keeping the group; (2) use a fresh consumer group

There aren't really any predictable performance metrics -- what's happening here is that the PDFMiner library we use to get page layouts is running some sort of inefficient algorithm to sort all the elements on each page of the PDF, so if you have pages with a lot of elements on them it will increase processing time exponentially.

The way to fix this would be to implement a more efficient algorithm inside PDFMiner's `process_page` function -- https://github.com/pdfminer/pdfminer.six/blob/master/pdfminer/pdfinterp.py#L840

In the meantime you could definitely write a program with `multiprocessing` that calls `pdf.load(n)` for each page in 8 processes.

This error is probably due to the requiretty setting in /etc/sudoers on the destination host, which is set by default on some systems:

> requiretty' If set, sudo will only run when the user is logged in to a real tty. When this flag is set, sudo can only be run from a login session and not via other means such as cron(8) or cgi-bin scripts. This flag is off by default.

This should be possible to work around on the client side as well, by specifying an ssh_config with the RequestTTY flag set to 'yes' or 'force', however the paramiko backend for testinfra does not currently recognize this key (RequestTTY).  I have worked around for now by using the ssh connection backend and pointing to an ssh_config with the RequestTTY flag set, e.g.

    RequestTTY force

Under a matching Hosts stanza.

Hi @bhumis!

There are two scroller widget options that you can change:

* [`scroller_jumpToHeader`](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-scroller.html#scroller-jump-to-header) - `true` by default - when `true`, it makes the table header jump into view when the table body is not scrolled to the top and while scrolling up the page.
* [`scroller_upAfterSort`](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-scroller.html#scroller-up-after-sort) - `true` by default - when `true`, the scroller automatically scrolls the inner window back to the top after sorting.

Try setting both of those options to `false`. If that still does not provide the desired behavior, please let me know.

I have one theory why that is. It's a guess but worth trying: 

I've noticed in the past that if the bucket size is very small compared to the time covered in the file, then the red/green bars are so thin that they can't be seen. 

To find out if that is the case, can you run the following command?

```
mloginfo /mongo/logs/mongodb.log
```

This will tell you start and end time of the file. The standard bucket size for the conchurn plot is 60 seconds. If the duration covered by the file is in the order of days or weeks, then the bucket size is definitely too small. (For example: 1 day = 24*60 minutes = 1440 buckets, that would probably be less than a pixel per bucket already, depending on the chart size).

You can change the bucket size in `mplotqueries` with the `--bucketsize` parameter. It's in seconds, so perhaps choose 3600 (= 1h buckets) or even larger depending on the duration covered by your log file.

```
mplotqueries /mongo/logs/mongodb.log --type connchurn --bucketsize 3600 --output 1stplot.pdf
```

Hope that fixes the issue. Let me know.

>I'm currently wondering about the color attributes/tags apart from `sysdata`.

AFAIK There are no formatter documentation yet. I'll give it a try.

You can replace `COLOR` in... `[\?color=COLOR]` with... 
* bad          // tied to `color_bad`
* good        // tied to `color_good`
* degraded // tied to `color_degraded`
* #ffccff // custom (full)
* #fcf // custom (short)
* ----- // color names that may or may not be added in the future... like `orange`, `silver`, `lime`, etc.

I see that `sysdata` uses only one `thresholds` number to colorize all parameters so that might be hard to configure because numbers could be clashing with each others.

You may have to use `[\?color=COLOR&show Hello]` sometimes only if there are no parameter to work with. There are more to it than just `colors`. We got `if`, max_length, `has_zero`, etc... More formatter options could be implemented eventually in the future. @tobes recently added `soft` not long ago to deal with empty parameters, eg `{artist}[\?soft - ]{title}` becomes `Loud Radio` instead of `- Loud Radio`.

See [test_formatter.py](https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/tests/test_formatter.py) for some good examples.

You may know about `color_{good, bad, degrade}`, but not `color = #ffccff`. That option should colorize the particular module pink... only if it's not being colorized by other `color_` options.

Anyhow, I hope that helps things.

Well regarding your first point, _ideally_ Faretricks wouldn't require users to write any code, just use the command-line "wizard" to create them semi-automatically.

Regarding the other point: if someone is (strangely) motivated enough to write malicious adapters for FFF and actively distribute them, they are also probably motivated enough to write a Python script or instruct a user to modify the plugin zip-files in the Calibre configuration directory. I mean, I get your worries, I'm just unsure if they represent a realistic threat model.

>  ...it would get stuck for a short time if one point in the list are not valid.

If the point in the configuration list isn't valid the application should have received an error PDU (like "unknown object") almost immediately and the prairie dog should have picked up the next request in the point queue.

> my way to do this now is to create one prairie dog for each points

This won't help much because the application will queue the requests coming down the stack by address and only have one outstanding at a time.  This is for applications that do not have their requests well coordinated (like a web server that is requesting a bunch of points from different devices all at the same time).  There is no way to know how many concurrent requests a particular controller can handle before it starts dropping them and in my experience even if a controller accepts multiple requests, it completes them in a serial order so the total time to satisfy all of them remains about the same.

Your application should behave the same as if you have one prairie dog for each field device, and to give each one its own point list.

> I have multiple objects and properties on same IP address.

If it supported by the controller, try using ReadPropertyMultiple which is more work to built the request and interpret the response, but will give you dramatically better throughput.  Your requests could be as simple as reading the magic `all` property.  Be aware that you could run up against a controller that would like to return everything at once but that would exceed the maximum APDU size of the networking layer and it doesn't support segmentation.

While the work is can be tedious, but you can also walk through a Wireshark capture and line up the requests and responses and see if you can find the bottleneck.  There are some applications in the [bacpypes-pcap](https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes-pcap) project that might help.

The line with `ttkthemes.ThemedStyle()` is indeed required, as it calls upon the `tk` attribute (which is an internal `Tcl`-interpreter for a `Tk` instance, which `Tkinter` automatically assigns to the most recently created `Tk` object if no alternative is provided in the kwargs). The current line you're using for that is not at all readable though, as a lot of stuff is happening under the hood that's not shown. It can also easily be broken if the code is not called in the order you expect, causing very weird behaviour that's hard to debug.

Moving on through your code: what you're doing is not how Python, nor this library, is meant to be used. You're modifying a normal `Tk` instance into something that has to resemble a `ThemedTk` instance by calling functions of the `ThemedTk` class on it. This is something that I cannot support if I ever decide to change stuff, as it's really fragile (PyCharm will give you a notification for a reason, if you don't use it, try it 😉 ). Trust me, I've tried that stuff too once and it was not a pleasent experience.

Something like this would suit your needs, I think:

```python
import tkinter as tk
from ttkthemes import ThemedStyle

def setup():
    root = tk.Tk() # Or your own custom class for that matter, or whatever 
                   # way you want to get your Tk instance
    style = ThemedStyle(root)
    style.set_theme("plastik")
```
That's the way the API is intended to work, and as it has been tested to work. The other option is a custom class that inherits from two other classes:

```python
import tkinter as tk
from ttkthemes import ThemedTk

class MyThemedCustomTk(NormalCustomTk, ThemedTk):
    # And add the right stuff, but an __init__ call to ThemedTk is still mandatory!
```

If, even after these two options, you would like to bypass all of the `ttkthemes` python code, you can (though I will not guarantee its compatibility with future versions) by doing what `ThemedTk` essentially does in its `__init__` function: calling upon a `tkinter.Tk.tk` Tcl interpreter.

> An unhandled exception does not, and has the potential to leak internal data which you may not want the client to see.

Thats a good point, I was missing it.

But actually I've meant simpler thing: on client-side, if you want to show some meaningful message to a user, you'll have to check both `message` and `data` fields (since latter could be missing; unhandled exceptions on server side do happen sometimes :scream:). This checking is especially awkward when you're using language with static typing, like I do.
Swapping `message` and `data` in case of unhandled exception would make it lot easier. Moreover, `message` field does not conform with [JSON API](http://jsonapi.org/format) anyways, so I suggest doing it. I could provide you with corresponding pull-request if you want.

Strings aren’t displayed in Apple Numbers in ‘constant_memory’ mode

In Workbook() constant_memory mode XlsxWriter uses an optimisation where cell strings aren’t stored in an Excel structure call “shared strings” and instead are written “in-line”.

原文如下：
>Currently when a standard library http client (the urllib , urllib2 , http , and httplib modules) encounters an https:// URL it will wrap the network HTTP traffic in a TLS stream, as is necessary to communicate with such a server. However, during the TLS handshake it will not actually check that the server has an X509 certificate is signed by a CA in any trust root, nor will it verify that the Common Name (or Subject Alternate Name) on the presented certificate matches the requested host.

The failure to do these checks means that anyone with a privileged network position is able to trivially execute a man in the middle attack against a Python application using either of these HTTP clients, and change traffic at will.

This PEP proposes to enable verification of X509 certificate signatures, as well as hostname verification for Python's HTTP clients by default, subject to opt-out on a per-call basis. This change would be applied to Python 2.7, Python 3.4, and Python 3.5.

Hi, yes, the problem is caused by a missing antiword executable. The problem is that the compiled antiword version for windows are very old (16-bit protected mode and 32-bit protected mode), those are from 2005 and has not been updated since. So is not possible to run antiword (hence manipulate .doc files with textract) unless antiword is recompiled for newer windows versions...

I finally moved on to linux and everything runs great. My advice is that if you really want to run textract on windows 10, the linux subsystem is a great alternative, I have tested installing textract inside the linux subsystem and works great. I had to install antiword manually Pillow by following [this](https://askubuntu.com/questions/547656/install-pillow-on-ubuntu-14-04) and to install antiword by typing this: `apt-get install antiword`.

Cons: As you are essentially running linux inside windows, you can access windows files (documents in our case), but you have to configure the python environment from scratch. Furthermore, I have experienced some slow disk-operations, but I guess it may change in the future.

[More info on linux subsystem inside windows](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/commandline/wsl/install_guide)

I do not know if this workaround is enough to make PR to the docs. Let me know :)

@juanpialbano A detailed information available here http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/#how-chatterbot-works

However i would like add some information here on learning process. 

1. Chatterbot is Retrieval-Based Bot , all retrieval-based bot are uses some [pre-defined responses](https://github.com/gunthercox/ChatterBot/wiki/ChatterBot-Corpus-Specification#data-representation-schema).

2. After that you have to [train](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/quickstart.html#training-your-chatbot) your bot. 

3.  The [training process](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/training.html#training) stores all  [statements ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html#statements) and [responses ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html#responses) into a [specified database](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/storage/index.html#storage-adapters).

4. And also forms a [statement-response-relationship ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html#statement-response-relationship)

5. When ever user inputs statements, the response selection process uses some [logic adapters](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/storage/index.html) to select proper response by [compare ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html#statement-comparison) each statement with inputted statement.

There are a few reasons why statements like `"Can I ask you a question?"` are stored when they have an empty `in_response_to` attribute.

1. Other data can be stored on the statement object. For example, statements can have an `extra_data` attribute that holds information generated by various adapters.
2. For data consistency. Every response has a corresponding statement that _can_ have responses.
3. From a performance perspective, it is useful to have it in case we need to list all statements known by the chat bot. If we did not do this, then we would need to traverse all statements and all statement-responses to list them.
4. Since the chat bot's database is essentially storing a graph data structure represented as the relationship between statements and responses, then it is useful to have the next statement in the graph, even if that statement has no known responses. This just tells us that there is more to learn.

I hope these help.

Hi klasko2, you raise a good point. This is actually the reason I originally didn't want to force value type conversion to the field type, and instead raise an exception. 

As you say, the reason here is that the default decimal length is 0, which is stated in the Readme. This has always been the case, but previous versions of pyshp simply ignored the decimal arg, and wrote the entire floating nr regardless, leading to possibly incorrect files. 

So the behavior is correct, but I agree with your point that since the 'F' type is float by definition it should default to using decimals>0. Also with the 'N' numeric type I think a case can be made as you suggest that most users would expect it to default to decimals rather than int. Defaulting to decimals is also less damaging because if people forget to specify field type then at least no information is lost. 

I am going to raise this as a separate improvement issue, and we can discuss there whether the default decimal should be changed to >0 and if so how many decimals. Also maybe a discussion of the default field size, and if that should be different for different data types.

Alright, so:

- It’s completely out of question to put attrs into the stdlib `ipaddress`-style and basically freeze it in time. 
- Going the simplejson/json route and adding it under a different name would make me rather sad, but at least people would be able to keep using `attrs` proper although the situation is confusing to many.
- Having a versioned and upgradeable instance of attrs part of the stdlib – leaving open how that’s achieved – is something I could agree to.

The last point opens a question though: one reason why core is interested in this is that they’d like to use it themselves within the standard library.  I’m sure it’s possible to achieve but there’s always the risk that an attrs update breaks the stdlib.

***

To address some of the concerns:
- **whimsical names**: if it’s too much for the stdlib, I’ve [already considered](https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs/issues/131) adding an `attrs` package from which you can import both `attrs`/`attrib` (serious business) as well as `attr` (`attr.s`/`attr.ib`).  This would be another impulse to follow through.  At this point I’m neutral on cute names but I’m annoyed about importing from `attr` .  The history is that `attrs` vastly outgrew it’s original scope; for better or for worse.
- `attrs`’s **Python 2 support** is an non-issue.  `attrs` was always a Python 3-first project and both Tin and I have mostly Python 3 in prod. If we have to compromise, we do it on the expense of Python 2.  The proposed usage of class annotations already [works in a proof of concept](https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs/issues/214) – I just want to take it as an opportunity to iron out some little warts we’ve accumulated (mostly how slots –  create new class with added  methods – vs non-slots classes – tack on methods – are created). 
- **serialization**: part of integrating class annotations will also be to make types a first class citizen of attributes.  @glyph has a working serialization lib in prod (not public yet as I understand), Tin’s cattrs is a thing too.  Things like `asdict` mentioned in the serialization ticket seem beside the point to me: what we need is unequivocal type data we can work on.   It’s well possible that this becomes an Python 3-only feature in attrs proper. #ohwell

Did I forget something?

OK, I am going to stop arguing against dict_factory.

Do we care about cycles in the structure? E.g. parent -> child -> parent?
Pickling supports this (and other sharing), but JSON does not. Since
asdict() has nothing to do with pickling but is the starting point for
JSON, I propose we needn't worry about it (it would also wreak havoc with
other supplied functions such as `__repr__` and `__eq__`). I do think we
should be clear about this somewhere in the PEP.

(One option I can think of would be to exclude the parent pointer by not
making it a field. Another would be to have a way to exclude it from
asdict() through a similar mechanism as used to exclude a field from
`__repr__`, `__init__` or `__cmp__`. Maybe we could use the `repr=False`
flag to mean that the field should be excluded from the dict? Or add an
extra `dict=True` flag to `field`?)

I'm still curious about the design of a protocol we could share with attrs.

`is_collection` should be available as an attribute on the `Item` object if the item is a collection. If it's absent, it's not a collection. For example:

    In [1]: from internetarchive import get_item
    
    In [2]: item = get_item('nasa')

    In [3]: item.is_collection
    Out[3]: True

If you try to access the attribute on a non-collection item, and the attribute is absent, it will throw an AttributeError exception. Now that I think of it, that's not very helpful. It should be set to False for non-collection items. I'll keep this issue open, so we can address that in a future release.

However, I think it might be easier for you to do the filtering in your query, so you don't even have to bother checking that:

    for item in search_items('collection:nasa AND NOT mediatype:collection').iter_as_items():
        item.download()

Or, from the command-line:

    ia download --search 'collection:nasa AND NOT mediatype:collection'

And, from the command-line with GNU Parallel:

    ia search 'collection:nasa AND NOT mediatype:collection' -i > itemlist.txt
    parallel 'ia download {}' < itemlist.txt

Currently, django-reversion does not use any top-level settings, and I'd like to keep it that way. Instead, I'd implement this feature at all levels of revision management:

1. A new `atomic=True` keyword parameter to `reversion.create_revision()`.
2. A new `atomic=True` keyword parameter to `reversion.views.create_revision()`.
3. A new `revision_atomic=True` class property on `reversion.views.RevisionMixin`.
4. A new `atomic=True` class property on `reversion.middleware.RevisionMiddleware`.

That way, the feature is uniform with all other reversion features, and controlled in the same way.

I'll commit documentation and fix the failing test based on the following rationale:

I'm reasonably sure that the failing test is actually the corner case I document inline.  What should occur during `loaddata` is a `create` that includes a version number.  For simple objects, this test didn't error.  When this affects an inherited object, you end up in the `_save_table` branch and eventually inside `_wrap_do_update` during a `create` operation.  Since the create includes a non-default version number, this was (incorrectly) getting treated as a non-concurrent update.  The new code realizes `loaddata` is a create, skips the checks, and creates the object.

Try to pass an appropriate `encoding` parameter to `get_port_name`.

> get_port_name(self, unsigned int port, encoding='auto')
>
>    Return the name of the MIDI input or output port with given number.
>
>   Ports are numbered from zero, separately for input and output ports. The number of available ports is returned by the get_port_count method.
>
>    The port name is decoded to a (unicode) string with the encoding given by encoding. If encoding is "auto" (the default), then an appropriate encoding is chosen based on the system and the used backend API. If encoding is None, the name is returned un-decoded, i.e. as type str in Python 2 or bytes in Python 3.

Stronghold relies on [django's authentication system](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/contrib/auth/). DRF is not a part of django itself, so no, stronghold doesn't know about its authentication system. You just want use the public decorator on your views.

In source, `stronghold.middleware` is the code path you're asking about:
https://github.com/mgrouchy/django-stronghold/blob/master/stronghold/middleware.py#L10

Stronghold smartly calls the user.is_authenticated method, which the [django docs says will always return False for an anonymous user](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/contrib/auth/#django.contrib.auth.models.AnonymousUser).

If you'd like to work on adding smart support for DRF authentication, that would be an interesting extended feature.

Depends on how your sending the metrics, size of  you application, machine is running on, etc...

Grouping the metrics together and pushing at an interval with reduce any overhead and allow you to get much higher throughput.

Based on how you have your code, you might want to use something like [statsd](https://github.com/etsy/statsd/wiki), You can then have it aggregate the metrics and then push into graphite.

Thread-safety isn't the only issue though I'm not sure whether there is a real problem with thread-safety, where do your doubts go?
A clear problem is `LogProcessingWorker.memory_cache` which is shared between the instances and so not thread-safe. This maybe could be easily solved using `threading.local()`.

There might be problems with the housekeeping of the worker threads, this needs deeper inspection and testing.

My initial intention was to store the worker thread as a class attribute as some sort of a singleton as I wanted to ensure a single worker thread for the whole application (to be precise: the whole process).
Your use case is clearly different.

Possible workaround:
As long as you use the same Logstash instance for both handlers, you could bind different formatter instances to the handlers and configure the formatters with different `message_type`s (or use tags) to later differentiate the events in Logstash while transmitting them over the same port with one worker thread.

Or if you don't want to install and setup a C compiler you can install conda/anaconda and then use the `conda` command to install it: `conda install -c conda-forge iteration_utilities` (or install it with conda in PyCharm)

Unfortunately there's no sort of default handling of JSON in deform...  I guess someone could create a widget, but editing JSON is very much a programmer thing and not really a web user thing.  If you have JSON, you really need to create your own widget that deals with the specific kind of data you're storing in the JSON field.

I was thinking more of the base case (not just JSON) for all field types that we don't recognize in ColanderAlchemy becoming a simple text field. JSON should work provided it's formatted correctly, but other types may not be able to properly handle a string formatted version.

Really, though, JSON needs to be handled by the developer... it's kind of beyond what ColanderAlchemy can reasonably do anything about.

Thank you for your comments, Joe. Please allow me to briefly explain in-line.

> See, in a production setup, you would have at least 2+ nodes behind a load balancer.

There are many, **many** production environments that utilize a single node without a load balancer.

> In a multi node environment you can not use local discs, but need to use a storage solution.

Each node presumably runs on a server that has a disk. A disk that can, and will, fill up if not monitored. Happens all the time.

> When it comes to memory, you get a different node, each time you GET the health check endpoint. Since the load balancer would randomly route the request to a different node.

Sure, but wouldn't the app developer want to know if a particular node is running out of memory?

> Besides that, the cluster should be able to detect unhealthy nodes and replace them automatically.

I think that makes a lot of assumptions about the average, everyday production environment that may not hold up to close scrutiny. Yes, there are some high-availability configurations that behave the way you describe. There are also many thousands of environments that do not behave that way.

This is a quickly hacked implementation which enables source maps for babel.

I added ``compile_source_to_file``, which replaces the following calls:

```python
compiled = self.compile_file(source_path)
compiled = self.postprocess(compiled, source_path)
self.write_output(compiled, source_path)
```

In babel I override this method and force source maps via the command line options. I also have to specify the input and output file as described in my previous post.

However that method would sacrifice the possibility to use ``postprocess``. Maybe it is possible to use a ``preprocess`` instead and then compile the file, since post processing the file may likely destroy the source map.

The way I did it is to have Ansible set it all up. I can tune the number of workers with simple variables. Ansible creates the SystemD service files. It creates the Nginx config. So they always match up. If I want 20 workers tomorrow I can just run it and know then Nginx config is right, the sockets are there, and the service files are there.

I have two worker groups A and B. When A is running and I want to deploy new code I spin up B and put A into a drain state. That way all new connections get the new code and anyone who is still connected can take their time disconnecting.

I have Ansible poll for 4 hours waiting for there to be 0 connections on the inactive worker group. If people haven't disconnected by then they get kicked off and usually automatically reconnect with Channel's websocket bridge.

When I want to deploy again I take A from offline and make it active and put B into drain - constantly switching between the two. This limits the amount of deployments you can do. Usually 1 a day without interrupting anyone that is in the middle of something.

```python
import time
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np

n = 40000

start = time.time()
for i in range(10000):
	x = mx.nd.arange(n)
	mx.random.shuffle(x, out=x)
	y = iter(x.asnumpy())
end = time.time()
print("mx elapsed time: ", end - start)

start = time.time()
for i in range(10000):
	x = np.arange(n)
	np.random.shuffle(x)
	y = iter(x)
end = time.time()
print("np elapsed time: ", end - start)
```
On i7-3770K CPU @ 3.50GHz, the result is
```
mx elapsed time:  3.1706936359405518
np elapsed time:  5.6994311809539795

On two Xeon(R) CPU E5-2680 v4 @ 2.40GHz, the result is
```
mx elapsed time:  2.679560661315918
np elapsed time:  6.299736976623535
```
As I increase `n`, the time ratio np / mx also increases. If `n` is smaller than 15000, np has shorter running time in i7. If `n` is smaller than 10000, np outperforms mx also in Xeon. This was reported nearly a year ago and still is this way in the latest SDK. Nearly every API accepts a `batch` argument but does not allow retrieving the success or failure responses, which eliminates almost all the utility of using batching.

For example, the signature for `Ad.api_get` is:

```python
    def api_get(self, fields=None, params=None, batch=None, pending=False):
```

So we can see that a batch object can be passed in to batch request multiple `Ad` objects, but later in the code we have...

```python
        if batch is not None:
            request.add_to_batch(batch)
            return request
```

The `add_to_batch` method is called without a `success` or `failure` callback as provided by `FacebookAdsApiBatch` therefore, it will be impossible to actually get the response from the `api_get` call, making it completely pointless.

You can't (or rather shouldn't) use zmq sockets to talk to regular tcp sockets. Both sides should be zeromq. Otherwise, you have to reimplement the extra zmq logic that the libzmq C++ library implements.

You can use pyzmq with asyncio:

```python
import asyncio
import zmq.asyncio
zmq.asyncio.install() # necessary in pyzmq 16, not 17
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
ctx = zmq.asyncio.Context()
s = ctx.socket(zmq.ROUTER)
msg = await s.recv_multipart()
```

A little background https://github.com/autozimu/LanguageClient-neovim/issues/21.

Basically the issue is because pyls write huge amount of logging messages to stderr, exhausting the stderr buffer. When using Popen to open pyls as subprocess, the piped stderr is buffered, and that buffer size usually has a limit of 4K.

So we could either pass `@hookspec(firstresult=True)` to the completions hook, meaning we'll use the first non-None result. Or we merge and try to filter duplicates.

I'd rather not have config to switch between them because it means the user needs to understand the pros/cons of each. Also, it's similar behaviour to firstresult=True, but less powerful since there's no fallback.

The disadvantage of first result (and your solution too), is that it becomes impossible for a plugin to add extra completions into the mix. Which was the point of plugins in the first place.

There is an easy way to get the tectonic region type, i.e the command `show ruptures_per_trt`,
Here is an example:

```bash
$ oq show ruptures_per_trt <calc_id>`

====================== ====== ==================== =========== ============ ============
source_model           grp_id trt                  num_sources eff_ruptures tot_ruptures
====================== ====== ==================== =========== ============ ============
as_model_emme_Iran.xml 0      Subduction Inslab    3           89           393,276     
as_model_emme_Iran.xml 1      Subduction Interface 3           97           36,774      
as_model_emme_Iran.xml 2      Deep Seismicity      16          152          687,247     
as_model_emme_Iran.xml 3      Stable Shallow Crust 30          160          963,941     
as_model_emme_Iran.xml 4      Active Shallow Crust 309         3448         3,397,401   
fs_model_emme_Iran.xml 5      Subduction Inslab    3           95           393,276     
fs_model_emme_Iran.xml 6      Deep Seismicity      16          146          687,247     
fs_model_emme_Iran.xml 7      Subduction Interface 18          300          38,786      
fs_model_emme_Iran.xml 8      Stable Shallow Crust 7,537       92           247,792     
fs_model_emme_Iran.xml 9      Active Shallow Crust 38,117      2504         1,921,509   
====================== ====== ==================== =========== ============ ============
```

From the rupture tag one can extract the source group ID and then the tectonic region type. For instance the tag `grp=09~ses=0001~src=active_546~rup=8604456-01` corresponds to the group 9 which is an Active Shallow Crust.

With the release of 1.4.0 of boto3, you now have the option to both ``io_chunksize`` and ``max_io_queue`` so for the environment where the network speed is much faster than the io speed you can configure it in a way to make io stop being the bottleneck: https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/customizations/s3.html#boto3.s3.transfer.TransferConfig

It is important to note that with the current configuration, the defaults should be suitable. Now the ``io_chunksize`` is 256KB, which seems to be a good default value as I have found in my testing and testing from others,  @gisjedi. For me with the current default configurations, boto3 achieves the same speed for downloads as the CLI for large downloads on larger instances.

Closing out issue as the defaults should now be resulting in better performance and the necessary configuration parameters related to io are now exposed to tweak to make the download faster if the results from using the defaults are still not as desired.

First, there's the question of what is a recipe. There are two answers so far.

The idea about mapping Git commands to the API. This is the approach of libgit2sharp, see https://github.com/libgit2/libgit2sharp/wiki/LibGit2Sharp-Hitchhiker%27s-Guide-to-Git (but they also state: *we currently lack proper documentation*, see https://github.com/libgit2/libgit2sharp/wiki#documentation).

In my opinion such approach would lead to duplicated information, both in the recipes and the reference, twice the work to maintain. The reference is already supposed to include examples to illustrate the API.

I agree with the @rmoehn description of what a recipe should be, the kind of stuff you find in a cookbook style book. So straightforward recipes should be removed. But if they contain any information not yet in the reference, then such information should be put in the reference first.

If you install libraries to non-standard locations, you need to tell the linker where to find the shared objects. It's possible that exporting `LIBGIT2_LIB` would have worked as well, but if you want dependencies to be kept elsewhere, exporting the linker configuration would be the way to go.

I discovered the problem, and the problem is that there is a bug with the vim.vm.device.VirtualDiskOption.CompatibilityMode object.  To be percise, the following does not set the backing.diskMode to be physical:

disk.backing.compatibilityMode = vim.vm.device.VirtualDiskOption.CompatibilityMode.physicalMode

To set the mode to physical you have to set it like this:

disk.backing.compatibilityMode = "physicalMode"

I hope this helps to prevent others from pulling their hair out like I did. ;)

This worked for me, using basic auth with a squid3 proxy:

```
auth_api.set_proxy('myproxy.local.net',
        port='8080',
        headers={
            "Proxy-Authorization":
            "Basic " + base64.b64encode(b"proxyUsername:proxyPassword").decode("utf-8")
        },)
```

Set the headers param in the `set_proxy` call, which expects a dictionary. Keys are HTTP Header names, and values are the associated header value. I tested this with python 3. You may need to tweak it for python 2.

This will work with a CONNECT proxy only. Duo's API code wants to directly validate the SSL connection to Duo's servers (instead of trusting the proxy to validate the upstream connection). HTTP CONNECT helps accomplish that.

If the connection between your API and proxy is (non-SSL) HTTP, be aware that this will put the proxy user's password on the network. The HTTPS connection end-to-end between the API client and Duo's server will still be secure, even with a (non-SSL) HTTP proxy.

Oh I see the problem:

            yield data_aggregation.tripCreatedOrChanged(request)

That's calling the handler function directly. The handler function has been decorated by TChannel to *always* return a Response object if the exception raised was an application exception (i.e., it was added under the `throws` section in the Thrift IDL). We have plans to make handler functions callable with request objects for tests (#144), but that's not yet supported.

If you do

    response = yield data_aggregation.tripCreatedOrChanged(request)

You'll see an object with class `DataAggregationService_userCreatedOrChanged_response` that has the `invalidArgument` field set to the exception.

In contrast Ruby/Bundler has

+ `gemspec` - Specification file for libraries (Equivalent to `setup.py`)
+ `Gemfile` - Desired dependencies (Equivalent to `Pipfile`)
+ `Gemfile.lock` - Locked dependencies, generated from `Gemfile` (Equivalent to `Pipfile.lock`)

For libraries that are consumed by others and published on the repository you would use `gemspec` for Ruby/Bundle and `setup.py`for Python. For projects (scripts, web backends, etc) you forego the `gemspec`/`setup.py` and instead use `Gemfile`/`Pipfile` and `Gemfile.lock`/`Pipfile.lock`. Libraries don't commit their lock files an depend on semver for transitive dependencies that are resolved at install-time. I think Pipfile is leaning more in this direction than JS (where both a package specification for a library and dependencies for a non-library project live in the same file).
I would add another minus against DSL. Too much flexibility can grow into [something monstrous](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/setup.py).

I think in general the whole idea and problem space behind Pipfile+Pipfile.lock et al could benefit from a real world narrative with better framing in standard industry terminology.

the `requirements.txt` form today provides a set of "requirement specifications" which are used in conjunction with other configurations (extended args in requirements.txt, cli args, config files) with tools like pip to both *resolve and install* requirements. the bigger idea behind a "Pipfile.lock" represents a broader concept known as a "frozen resolve". the idea behind a "frozen resolve" is to break the resolution (e.g. the translation of requirements specifications like "requests" or "requests>=1.0" against other constraints - interpreter type, platform, abi, etc - into a set of URLs, based on their metadata) apart from the retrieval (where possible) and installation/bundling/etc of the resolve product (e.g. fetching "http://my-file-server/requests/requests-2.11.1-py2.py3-none-any.whl" at some sha and installing that into a venv/pex/par etc).

because resolves against open ended constraints can be heinously non-deterministic, the idea of frozen resolves has emerged as a way to bring sanity into larger production environments. rather than checking in open ended constraints that when resolved lead to non-deterministic behavior at e.g. the same git sha but at different time periods - frozen resolves lead to retrieval of consistent products (assuming an immutable file store) with a consistent installation/bundling result. more importantly, frozen resolves can never spontaneously fail at the *resolve* phase due to unsatisfiable constraints. this effectively surfaces resolution issues directly to developers at commit time as a first class development step rather than later as package constraints shift due to unpinned versions (or transitive unpinned versions).

so effectively, a `Pipfile.lock` is a "frozen resolve". I feel like in particular the name `Pipfile.lock` seems like a huge verbiage mistake. this isn't a lockfile at all. it's an entirely new product type that is the intermediate step to deterministic python software builds.

### Ideal approach:
Use conditional analysis to find independent signals, for which the top is the peak and the rest is masked.  This isn't going to happen soon, so for now we just try to approximate it in an explainable way.

### Triangley approach:
Take the best variant.  It masks all variants in a triangle (on the Manhattan plot) below it.  Then repeat.  After assigning 200 peaks, mask everything below the pvalue of the 200th peak.

eg, if it has pval 1e-12, it'll mask pval>1e-11 for ±100kb, pval>1e-10 for ±200kb, pval>1e-9 for ±300kb, pval>1e-10 for ±400kb, etc.

That way, if two neighboring variants have the same p-value, neither will mask the other.  If a variant on a shoulder is quite significant, it'll be shown but not otherwise.

For each peak, we can compute the range within which it masks variants, and how many significant variants it masks.

It doesn't seem like the benefits of this approach outweigh the confusion of explaining it.

The page is declared to by XHTML 1.1 but in fact is not XHTML so the parsers get confused. As can be checked with simple XPath selectors like `/*` there are three root elements in the parsed tree so only XPath `//` queries can match all elements in the document, while parsel doesn't use `//` for CSS selectors, effectively using only the first root element. 
You can replace `sel.css('.foo')` with `sel.xpath('../' + sel._css2xpath('.foo'))`, I don't know of any better options for such cases.

We could use `sys.path.insert(0, ...)` instead of `sys.path.append(...)` to make your stuff get imported first, but that could lead to weird side effects if another package down the line tried to use the `email` package in the standard library.

There are workarounds for importing modules based on absolute paths, but they're horrible to get to work across Python versions because they keep changing `importlib`.

Yes, I don't think agavepy needs to support walking the Authorization code flow. I think the assumption would be that if you are using `authoritzation_code` you are using it as a library in a larger framework, and therefore would be able to handle walking the flow. But there doesn't seem to be a way to pass agavepy the code, etc., to get a token. Right now I am making the call to `/token` directly with requests.

I think a lot of the use cases that agavepy covers right now are great for "interactive use", e.g. at a python prompt or in a notebook. But using it as a library in a web application, for instance, there are a few quirks. The two that I can think of off-hand are support for additional `grant_type`s and the auto-token refresh. (Right now we are not taking advantage of token refresh, either, because it would result in the token being refreshed without the webapp knowing.)

C++11 has atomic operations that are portable, and not just some gcc implementation details:

    static std::atomic_int x = 1
    x += 1   // no race condition

C11 has similar operations in <stdatomic.h>, just uglier syntax.

Setting variables may or may not be atomic depending on the CPU. Eg on armeabi-v7a (basically all smartphones) you need a special ASM instruction to atomically set a 64-bit int which the compiler does not necessarily emit for a C/C++ assignment.

Also, _maybe_ there should be some work done to clarify that the mode ```InferenceType.TemporalAnomaly``` is _always_ used in _addition_ to predictions as well? Since the OPF _always_ does predictions, when Anomaly detection is also configured, it should be understood that it is always in addition to predictions and not _instead of_ predictions. (not mutually exclusive).

Just an update - I made a wrapper script to keep it running, and while I was at it, I logged times it restarted.  Here is the wrapper script

```
#!/bin/sh

touch slap.log

while true; do
  TIMESTAMP=`date '+%D %T'`
  echo "*** HueSlapper starting at $TIMESTAMP ***" >> slap.log
  ./hueslapper
  echo EXIT STATUS WAS $? >> slap.log
done

exit 0
```

Right, I understand that this is at best a bit weird in Python 2, but it's relatively easy to work around here and IMO it's useful to do so.

`unicode_literals` can be smelly in library code, since it can easily introduce API changes.  I wouldn't advocate using it without caution.  However, in leaf application code it can be a very useful way to make progress on transitioning.  In particular, the strategy I'm following in Launchpad (which is where I ran across this) is to first change the tests to use `unicode_literals` (watching out for the handful of cases that really need bytes instead), and make sure everything works there; once that works, it will be much easier to change the actual application code; and once we have appropriate versions of all our dependencies we can switch to py3-only.  I don't think this strategy runs afoul of the usual arguments against `unicode_literals`.  So far this is the first non-trivial problem I've run into.

@pseudotensor  @arno
However the primary driving force for the Jay format is not that of efficiency (for "normal" use cases efficiency gains are expected to be minimal). 

The main reason I want Jay to be implemented is that of stability. As we have seen in many issues already, the architecture that relies on having thousands or tens of thousands of memory map regions to be opened at once is just too fragile. We have so far found ways to deal with the problems and implement some stop-gap measures, but those measures are not a substitute for a reliable solution.

To be more specific, the measures that are currently in-place are the following: (1) if a memory-map fails due to a system limit, we *unmap* some of the older mem-mapped file, hoping that that file won't be needed at least in the near future; (2) when a memory allocation fails in some other part of the system, try to do the same unmapping trick, because apparently regular memory allocations sometimes require to memmap something internally. Neither of these is a reliable solution: the first one is built on hope, the second works only for memory allocations done in datatable, but memory allocations done in any other part of the system (in C++ core, in Python, in other libraries, etc) are simply not aware of it.

The concrete example(s) when the current system fails is any situation when you have many columns and you need to access them all at once, probably in a row-by-row fashion. Such use cases could be: writing the frame into a csv file, performing data aggregation for vis-data-server, converting the entire frame into pandas/ numpy, passing the frame to xgboost, etc.

With Jay I expect most of these problems would go away, simply because instead of tens of thousands of memmap regions, you'll have only tens of them. And that will make all the difference in the world.

@tagliala

On browsers rendering with DirectWrite, the default behavior is to allow full subpixel precision rendering of a font at sizes of 21px or greater. And the icons in the font set look *fine* at those sizes, if occasionally somewhat blurred.

The problem is that at pixel sizes below 21px, browsers apply the hinting embedded in the font, which in FontAwesome 4.2 and up is just utterly, utterly broken. This problem is **not limited to brand icons**. It affects all the icons in the set, but is just more noticible on some icons than on others.

You can, infact, work around the poor rendering fidelity due to the broken hinting by setting a css rotation small enough to remain visually undectable, but large enough to force the affected browsers (IE, Firefox, Chrome) to start rendering fonts with full sub-pixel geometric precision.

Here's a snap of the **unfixed** icon in the searchbox on FontAwesome's own icon overview page, as seen in Firefox on Windows 7 with Direct2D and DirectWrite enabled:
![Unfixed](http://s28.postimg.org/xiwz97s4t/no_fix.png)

And here's the same, with a `transform:rotate(0.001deg)` added to the `.fa` class via the developer tools:
![Fixed](http://s28.postimg.org/x4vn9m819/fix.png)

Notice the difference there?

After testing this further, I think I have a better idea of the behavior you've alluded to, and if I am correct I can push a fix.

Firstly, here is an example of a valid manual submission through the api browser. You may have inputted something like this in your testing `{"extra_vars":{"z":7,"x":9}}`. Here is an example of an input that works, being passed from tower-cli to the server `{'extra_vars': '{"y": 2, "z": 4}'}`. FYI, this will not work if you manually enter it into the api browser. This will be produced by tower-cli if you run it with flags formatted like this `--extra-vars z=4 --extra-vars y=2`. So again, that case works. But we don't want to have to use 2 flags. Say you want to combine these into one extra-vars flag. That would correctly work with the syntax `--extra-vars '{"y": 2, "z": 4}'`. However, I see how this is very fragile syntax.

Also, I had been under the impression that Tower would accept key=value pairs or yaml on its own, and thus it would be best to pass on extra-vars as-is and let Tower sort it out. This is how tower-cli had always worked before 2.3.0 anyway. Right now, I can not get it to accept any type of input other than json, and this is a problem. As it is, we don't touch the user input if there are not multiple sources of extra-vars to combine, and I would claim that is the source of the problems you have seen. To change this, we would simply disable the special case and always re-parse the user input.

Ants,

That makes sense.  It's also pointless to promote a node if there is no candidate sync rep, since that node will still not be able to accept synchronous writes, so that all works.

What I'm still concerned about is maintaining the list of candidate nodes, particually in a "rolling blackout" situation.  While you've eliminated a race condition by saying only the master can edit the list of nodes, there's still an inherent danger if the master is in the process of adding and removing nodes from the list and then goes dark (this is not a new problem, Cassandra and similar databases have a lot of logic devoted to this issue).

Je suis d'accord en théorie, et en même temps :
- un code plus aéré est aussi plus long, et plus "diffus" (a é r e r  n ' e s t  p a s  f o r c é m e n t  p l u s  l i s i b l e)
- le coté repoussant/plaisant est très personnel

Par expérience il est très difficile d'arriver à un consensus sur ce genre de question, justement parce que les préférences personnelles, habitudes et expériences des uns et des autres prennent de l'importance, et peuvent cliver.

Donc la décision se prend en général soit :
1/ par un argument d'autorité : c'est le maintainer, le plus gros commiteur, le plus ancien commiteur, le chef de projet/d'équipe/le manager qui décide
2/ par un argument historique "on a toujours fait comme ça" : c'est le style utilisé jusqu'à présent qui doit être conservé (pas toujours facile quand le style actuel n'est pas cohérent dans tout le dépôt)
3/ par un argument "code is law" : tel ou tel outil est adopté par la communauté/répandu/pratique/"non-compromising" (sans configuration possible, donc sans discussion possible)

- un outil automatisé (je pense qu'on est d'accords sur ce point)
- un outil adopté (ou en cours d'adoption ?) par une grande partie de la communauté, pour que ceux qui lisent le code aient de grandes chances d'avoir déjà vu ou travaillé sur du code formaté de la même manière
- avec une configuration inexistante ou minimale pour que les autres projets qui ont adopté le même outil soient formatés de la même manière

Herbert is back from Europe. We all had a discussion about this today and have resolved that a new method to the MementoClient class is the best path forward. We suggest the name get_location_from_timegate and a call signature of the form: ```` mc.get_location_from_timegate(request_uri, accept_datetime) ```` where request_uri is a URI-R and accept_datetime is obviously Accept-Datetime This function would then perform no checks on the URI-R, and just capture the first response from the location header presented by the TimeGate. It would also perform no checks against the URI returned from the location header, leaving that up to the discretion of the calling code. We would prefer that response be a Python dictionary in the format of the JSON API (http://timetravel.mementoweb.org/guide/api/). In this case, the output would just contain something like the following: ```` { "original_uri": "http://www.example.com/doc1.html", "timegate_uri": "http://timetravel.mementoweb.org/timegate/http://www.example.com/doc1.html", "mementos": "closest": { "datetime": "", "http_status_code": "", "uri" : [ "http://web.archive.org/web/19700101000000/http://www.example.com/doc1.html" ] } } ```` The value for the TimeGate is specified when the object is instantiated using the existing timegate_uri. There is no datetime or HTTP status code present in the JSON output, because this information is acquired by making a request to the URI-M. Also, in creating get_memento_info, we created additional methods for acquiring information throughout the process, that you may find useful in HTTP playback. Existing functions that may be helpful: * get_native_timegate_uri, used for sites whose URI-R headers contain their own timegates; takes a URI-R and an Accept-Datetime; then makes a request and reads the headers * is_timegate, used to determine if the URI-G specified does actually specify a TimeGate; takes a Python response object to avoid additional requests; will use the response object if present, otherwise sends a request to the URI-G * is_memento, used to determine if the URI points to a Memento; takes a URI-M and a Python response object to avoid additional requests; will use the response object if present, otherwise sends a request to the URI-M * __prepare_memento_response, used to generate the response in the JSON API format Is the new proposed method and the existing available functions helpful for the use case described?

We want the ability to communicate with a local proxy without having to speak TLS (it's kind of silly to have to do TLS with a local process).

There are two timestamps possible on a message:

- `message.timestamp` is in the `attributes` of the message, and must therefore be set by the topic:  setting those is controlled by the `topic.timestamp_messages` attribute, which defaults to `False`.
- `message.service_timestamp` is set by the back-end, and parsed from the `publishTimestamp` field of the pulled message resource.

To activate the first kind:

```python
from gcloud.pubsub.client import Client
client = Client(project='...')
topic = client.topic('${TOPIC}', timestamp_messages=True)
t.create()
s = t.subscription('${SUBSCRIPTION}')
s.create()
t.publish('test data')
ack_ids = []
for ack_id, m in s.pull():
    ack_ids.append(ack_id)
    print(m.data, m.timestamp, m.service_timetamp)
sub.acknowledge(ack_ids)
```

On my box, the previous example yields:

```
(datetime.datetime(2016, 9, 7, 20, 42, 18, 810563, tzinfo=<UTC>), 'test_data', None)
```

That tells me that for some reason the back-end is not setting the `publishTimestamp` field in the message resource it is returning, but that the timestamp attribute set by the publishing application *is* being set.

'EU'

I also got an error with boto installation in Python 3 on Google Dataproc instances running Debian 8.
I initialize a Dataproc cluster, install Python 3 and install google-compute-engine package with pip...

```
apt-get install -y python3 python3-dev python3-pip
pip3 install -v google-compute-engine
```

when I try to import boto in python 3 ...
`python3 -c "import boto"`

I get the following error:

 File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/boto/plugin.py", line 75, in _import_module
    return imp.load_module(name, file, filename, data)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/imp.py", line 235, in load_module
    return load_source(name, filename, file)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/imp.py", line 171, in load_source
    module = methods.load()
  File "/usr/share/google/boto/boto_plugins/compute_auth.py", line 18, in <module>
    import urllib2
ImportError: No module named 'urllib2'

After a long time of investigation, I guessed the problem. Boto saves its config in /etc/boto.cfg .
In Dataproc instances, the [Plugin] section of the config file is as follows, pointing to a directory with Python 2.7 code (which refers urllib2, not available in Python 3)

[Plugin]
plugin_directory = /usr/share/google/boto/boto_plugins

The  /etc/boto.cfg file is usually managed using scripts, so I just included this command in my bash script just after  google-compute-engine package install, to update /etc/boto.cfg with the correct plugin_directory (/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/google_compute_engine/boto)

`python3 -c "from google_compute_engine.boto.boto_config import BotoConfig; BotoConfig()"`

Just sharing in the case anyone runs into the same problem involving this setup: Dataproc (Image version v1.0), Python3 and boto.

So I dove into ze code and this is a multilayered problem. The trivial issue that causes this test to fail on the second go-around is because we're not passing --cache-ttl in the test. This works:

```
$ rm -rf ~/.pex
$ pex --version
pex 1.2.2
$ pex thrift thrift==0.9.1 -o test.pex
$ pex thrift thrift==0.9.1 -o test.pex
Could not satisfy all requirements for thrift:
    thrift, thrift==0.9.1
$ pex --cache-ttl=120 thrift thrift==0.9.1 -o test.pex
$
```

The reason it works with cache-ttl is because in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/resolver.py#L249 it resolves the packages found in the cache for the unbounded thrift resolvable if cache-ttl is set.

Note that in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/resolver.py#L246 if the resolvable is exact, i.e. thrift==0.9.1 and it finds it in the cache, it always uses the cache.

So, if cache-ttl is unset, for the unbounded `thrift` resolvable, it finds a bunch of SourcePackages on pypi, for the `thrift==0.9.0` resolvable, if it finds 0.9.0 in cache, it ALWAYS returns the cached versions and then in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/resolver.py#L58 it does a set intersection and finds *no common packages* and returns the empty set.

So, in the case of --cache-ttl being unset, you can either:

1. Set --cache-tll, and this will work until --cache-ttl expires, and then it won't work until you remove the cached 0.9.0 version
2. Remove the behavior of always returning the cached artifact if the resolvable is exact. I would favor this, because if --cache-ttl is set and the ttl of the artifact is within the ttl, it will still return the cached artifacts for both the unbounded and the exact resolvables.

However, I still think we have an identity crisis. Ultimately, I think it's a problem that this:

```
SourcePackage(u'https://pypi.python.org/packages/e7/9e/a1e13b5c3cb24f160fc34f589baf874fcb53951ec54895a7aa101cc39056/thrift-0.9.0.tar.gz#md5=600ad4d76bc87d3e0c8570557d60ae65')
```

Is not considered the same as this:

```
SourcePackage('file:///Users/nate/.pex/build/thrift-0.9.0.tar.gz')
```

Potential Solutions:

1. Hash on the name and version in the Package class

```
diff --git a/pex/package.py b/pex/package.py
index f786729..3520725 100644
--- a/pex/package.py
+++ b/pex/package.py
@@ -55,6 +55,12 @@ class Package(Link):
       cls._HREF_TO_PACKAGE_CACHE.store(href, package)
     return package

+  def __hash__(self):
+      return hash((self.name, self.version))
+
+  def __eq__(self, other):
+      return hash(self) == hash(other)
+
   @property
   def name(self):
     return NotImplementedError
```

The problem with this approach is that it's now order dependent, the set intersection can return the remote SourcePackage or the local SourcePackage depending upon which side of the intersection you're on.

2. Don't use intersection, create a comparitor based on name, value and maybe package type (prefer Wheels/Eggs over SourcePackages and Local over Remote) and loop through the two sets applying it.

Ultimately this bites us in pants land, we have no control over transitive 3rdparty dependencies and are often bit at build time by this which creates non-deterministic builds. We could disable cache but that would slow our build times unacceptably.

In the meantime I'm going to probably go for option 1, patch our pex with it in our cheeseshop and have it run on our build environment for a time to test it out.

Totally agree that the context-dependence of `:` as implemented in this PR right now is not something I want, but this string could be almost anything else we wanted it to be (for example, we could use the empty string instead, which would be *highly* analogous to the way you can do e.g. `a/b` and have that expand to `a/b:b`). The choice of representation shouldn't be context-dependent, and it doesn't need to be.

> Out of interest, how do you feel about the fact that `path/to/target` can be used as an alias to `path/to/target:target` in any BUILD file?

As someone with *very* little experience maintaining BUILD files and target naming across a monorepo over time, I like being able to depend on `path/to/target`, and I think it helps encourage 1:1:1 by making it clearer when 1:1:1 is being used, and making it "easier" to depend on a target which follows 1:1:1 (because I can get the target spec just by doing `os.path.dirname(source_file)` in my head).

I've been going back and forth in my head about whether not exposing a way to depend on the default target, whatever it's named, is an artificial restriction. I think now I am landing on the side of: no, if there are enough targets that it's not clear which one is the default one then adding a name to all of them seems like a reasonable approach to me, and not super artificial because if it's not clear what the default target is I don't think we should be making it easier to depend on it (this could lead to users depending on the slightly wrong thing). That would mean we wouldn't want to merge this change.

Hi Villu,

Thank you for your reply.

Here I am trying to implement an algorithm brought by [this paper](https://users.cs.fiu.edu/~chens/PDF/ICDM03_WS.pdf).

Technically speaking, `dist = X * X / self.singular_values_` is not a distance. It is a `p` dimension vector, where `p` is the number of `singular_values_`.

`major_component` is the sum of the first `q` element of `dist`
`minor_component` is the sum of the last `r` element of `dist`

`major_component` and `minor_component` will be both used as score. If either of the two are greater than the threshold, we predict a sample as anomalous.

If connected with a `DecisionTreeClassifier()`, `major_component` and `minor_component` will be used as feature.

Does this make sense?

Model verification is most useful with supervised learning methods such as regression and classification, where there is a "clear" target field to be checked.

Clustering is an unsupervised learning method. As the target field is missing, then something else needs to be checked, such as the distance of the verification data record to a (sub)set of clusters (in PMML speak, "cluster affinities").

> The same problem applies to functions.

Functions are easy since they either have a channel string parameter or not. And, this cannot change over time since that would break code. If the function started off without a channel string and then the driver team decided they needed one, they would have to create a completely new function.

We do have information about whether a given attribute is channel based or not. However, when @marcoskirsch and I were discussing whether to use that information, we decided not to because:

1. Is it correct for all attributes? Based on other inconsistencies we saw, we were not confident of this
1. Is it consistent for all devices in the driver? I.e. will it be channel based for device 5xxx and device based for 5yyy
1. What happens when that changes over time? I.e. right now it is device based, but when the PXIe 5zzz is released and the driver team makes it channel based. That ends up being a source breaker even if the customer is still using that attribute on the PCI 5xxx
1. What about attributes that are repeated capabilities based but not channel based?

It does solve how to deal with NI-DMM's lack of channels though while allowing the others to be consistent with NI-DAQmx and NI-XNET. All of the NI-DMM attribute have `'channel_based': 'False'`.
