The concern here was that quite a lot of twisted.web code would grab the backing transport directly, which breaks with HTTP/2 (because you can't just write bytes onto the transport and expect that to work).  The goal, then, was to pass all writes through the channel, which "owns" the transport.

Clearly, Autobahn was doing what the rest of twisted.web did (which was just grab the transport out of the request, without considering what the channel might want to do). Again, Autobahn can just grab the transport out of the channel: that should work on all Twisted versions.

The tokenizer used by the constituency parser predictor is defined here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4326b59b18c5f3f1eb29b1b3dfe330660d292d39/allennlp/predictors/constituency_parser.py#L67

To switch that tokenizer, you have a couple of options:

1. If you're running the predictor in code (not from the commandline), you can just overwrite the tokenizer that's there.  Something like `predictor._tokenizer = JustSpacesWordSplitter()` after you create the predictor.

2. If you're running from the commandline, you need to write your own predictor, switching the tokenizer.  This is pretty simple - just copy the code for the existing predictor, change the name that's registered, and change the tokenizer (and be sure to pass the `--include-package` flag to the `allennlp` command).  You also don't need all of the stuff dealing with hierplane and styles, as that's getting the output ready to display in the demo.

A slightly more satisfying solution would be to make our predictor configurable, either by taking the tokenizer as a constructor parameter, so you can configure it in code without overwriting anything, or by adding some kind of `from_params` method for predictors.  We don't have any mechanism for the latter at this point; it might be helpful, but might also be more work (and more complex) than it's worth.

Did you consider spawning (short-lived) sub-processes only when
necessary, instead of having a rigid set of processes with pipelines
for them to communicate with each other?  For example, whenever the
DevP2P code receives a new block, we could easily spawn a sub-process
calling a function to import the block; something like this:

`
executor = concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor()
await loop.run_in_executor(executor, import_block_fn)
`

I believe this approach would give us more flexibility, but it should
also be simpler to implement and we wouldn't need to supervise each of
the long-running processes started by trinity to make sure they're
always available.

>Does this extend to tests

Been wondering the same. My personal preference is "yes" but I'm ok to have tests be treated as an exception to this rule if there's some pushback on this.

>Is it ok to not annotate `self`, `cls`,

Yes, in fact this is treated as an exception by `mypy` anyway. So even if we use `--disallow-incomplete-defs` (which we do) omitting type annotations for `self` or `cls` is allowed and I believe is the preferred way of writing defs.

>and possibly the return type of `__init__`

That on the other hand does not seem to be possible as `mypy` always expects us to specify the return type for `__init__` as `None` leaving us with an error if we don't

```shell
error: The return type of "__init__" must be None
```

>Is there some kind of canonical style guide for more edge cases like this?

Not really a style guide but the closest thing to that that I'm aware of is the official [Cheat Sheet](http://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/cheat_sheet_py3.html).

Also I consider the [mypy codebase](https://github.com/python/mypy/blob/master/mypy/checkstrformat.py) itself to be a nice reference but keep in mind that the mypy code base is written in a Python 3.5 compatible way which means some of the things can actually be written nicer for projects that do not need to be Python 3.5 compatible (e.g Trinity / P2P)

I'll take this on, at least the framework. While working on web3 & eth-tester, another thing became apparent: if the block that you want to test against has no gas left in the block, then the current approach becomes impossible. You run out of gas while executing.

If we had really nailed the naming, I don't think there would have been so much discussion on it in #481.

Open questions:

1. Do we always use the ABI type name exactly, or do we pluralize `function` and `event`?
2. Do we allow invoking anything without parentheses? (like `myContract.fallback.transact()` or `myContract.function.myMethod.transact()`)

Maybe this alternative would feel cleaner?
```
myContract.function_myMethod().transact()
myContract.event_myEvent().transact()
myContract.constructor().transact()
myContract.fallback().transact()
```

That answers these questions this way:

1. We always use the ABI type name exactly, because plural reference doesn't fit anymore.
2. We always require explicit parentheses to invoke the function.

@hainm yes, there's a new compiler for MacOS too.  The fix for them is identical.  Those packages are:

* python-2.7.14-hd74e306_15.tar.bz2
* python-2.7.13-hdada7c8_15.tar.bz2
* python-3.5.4-h821eb87_14.tar.bz2
* python-3.6.2-h9e63aee_14.tar.bz2

The new mac compilers are the clang_osx-64 and clangxx_osx-64 packages.

The recipe for the new mac compilers are at https://github.com/AnacondaRecipes/aggregate/tree/master/llvm-compilers-feedstock/recipe

They do switch from libstdc++ (from gcc) to libc++ (from llvm/clang).  Keeping these consistent within a package ecosystem is extremely important, and conda-forge has demonstrated to us that libc++ is the right way to go on Mac.  The main reason for confusion before now has been that people were using the gcc (version 4.8.5) package, which had gcc, g++ and gfortran all in one.  They mostly used this when they needed fortran, but as a side effect, it introduced a lot of libstdc++ dependencies, and those caused problems.  The gfortran package on mac is now totally separate from gcc, and there's no longer any reason to use the gcc package on mac - prefer the clang packages I mentioned above (and ideally, use conda-build 3's ``{{ compiler('c') }}`` to use them).

I do think the real problem here is that conda-forge and defaults don't agree on what the metadata should say; and I thnk there are some objective problems with both channels' choices in the `traitsui` case.

anaconda.org treats a channel as a valid channel regardless of *subdir*.  For `kalefranz` as a channel, I know it's valid and legitimate channel because https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz/linux-64/repodata.json or https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz/ANY/repodata.json reply 200 with valid json.  However, `kalefranz2` *isn't* a valid channel.  https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz2/linux-64/repodata.json 

In conda I need to be able to make a similar distinction.  I'd like to do so without having to check for a 200 response to all known `subdir`s.  Especially as we add additional subdirs for power, arm, bsd, solaris, aix, etc etc.  As `noarch` is the universal common `subdir`, thats the one that makes sense to require and check.

With the new configuration engine, and to further enable enterprise users, I've added a configuration concept of `custom_channels`.  This lets sysadmins configure custom named channels just like you'd have on anaconda.org or an alternately configured `channel_alias`, but for completely custom locations.  It also helps with migrating the *locations* of channels from one url/dns to another.  Much of the history here has to do with this long-standing https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3267.

Validation at build time seems like something ``conda-build`` must absolutely do. However, some sort of package validation at installation time seems necessary to me, for the simple fact that a package manager should refuse to install unsupported package formats or corrupt packages. A package may be corrupt for many different reasons - a bug in the build system, bad post-build manipulation of the build artifact, or a dodgy download. Unfortunately the only objective way to decide if a missing file is an actual error is to ask the package maintainers. I would therefore suggest that conda should take a conservative approach and refuse to install packages with inconsistent metadata.

Your best bet would be to spin up a second, parallel client with a relatively short timeout (before the default) and use that to execute the forceMerge, knowing that it will fail.  If you only have one index to forceMerge, this is your "best effort."

It gets significantly more complicated, though, if you have several indices per day to forceMerge.  This is why I outlined the scenario above, and what you'd have to do to make sure it detected when an index had completed the forceMerge, so it could schedule the next.

I emphatically do not recommend trying to forceMerge more than one index at a time.  Yes, the API allows you to pass multiple indices to a forceMerge command.  The I/O load on the cluster could easily become crippling to ongoing indexing, merging, and querying operations.

This is another manifestation of this issue that I opened against the Python storage SDK months ago: https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/issues/220.

While we can do this in the CLI, it seems to me that the SDK is the appropriate place to split these functions. Any customer that tries to use the `get/set_container_acl` methods is going to experience the same issue. While I understand that they are tied together in the service, no one would expect setting the container permission to wipe their ACL or adding an ACL entry to wipe their container permissions. They are logically completely separate.

Separating these service calls would also work, but it would be just as acceptable (and easier) to simply have the SDK implement the workaround instead of asking every client application do so.

Hi @Fale 

Thanks for the feedback, but we do not plan to merge all packages into one.
Each endpoint is managed by different service team that didn't have the same deadlines. For instance, it's not because the Batch team made a breaking change, that it's worth updating a big package including Compute and Resources. Actually, most of our users are using 2 or 3 packages (like Compute+Network) and don't want to install the whole services on their machine (size on disk + time to install).
In addition, this is a consistent experience across language.
We are already working with Debian to provide one "azure" package with a frozen state of several packages and we're happy so far with that solution. We will update in a near future the "azure" meta-package, so you'll be able to install one package with one command if you want, but giving the opportunity to other people to precisely chose what they want.

FWIW, I've found a workaround for installing the `cryptography` package on Azure WebJobs.

`pip` will only install wheels with an ABI code of "`none`" during Web App deployment:
```
import pip
print(pip.pep425tags.get_supported())
[09/28/2016 17:08:30 > 775106: INFO] [('cp34', 'none', 'win32'), ('cp34', 'none', 'any'), ('cp3', 'none', 'any'), ('cp33', 'none', 'any'), ('cp32', 'none', 'any'), ('cp31', 'none', 'any'), ('cp30', 'none', 'any'), ('py34', 'none', 'any'), ('py3', 'none', 'any'), ('py33', 'none', 'any'), ('py32', 'none', 'any'), ('py31', 'none', 'any'), ('py30', 'none', 'any')]
```
Many modern packages in PyPI -- e.g. `cryptography==1.5.2` -- don't provide wheels with this ABI tag. Fortunately, it appears all will be well if the wheel file is renamed to have the "`none`" ABI tag.

I'll assume that you are already [deploying your Web App from a git repo](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/web-sites-python-configure/). Here are the steps to get `cryptography` installed:

1. Download the appropriate wheel for your Web App's Python version and the `win32` platform from [the `cryptography` page on PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/cryptography) into your Web App's repo.
2. Rename the file to change the ABI tag to "`none`", e.g.
    ```
    cryptography-1.5.2-cp34-cp34m-win32.whl =>cryptography-1.5.2-cp34-none-win32.whl
    ```
3. Repeat steps 1 and 2 for the [`cffi` package](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/cffi).
4. Add entries at the top of the `requirements,txt` file containing the filenames of the wheels.
5. Deploy via git.

I believe that'd be sufficient, though there are a few more steps I have taken (generating and including wheels for `azure-common`, `azure-nspkg`, and `azure-storage` via `pip wheel <package name>`, including other dependencies as wheels) that might also be necessary.

I did some reading, and there doesn't seem to a name that defines the U.S. style. Based on the Wikipedia [article on street addresses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Address_(geography)), here's [a spreadsheet with number + street address formats highlighted in yellow](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1hU0408yL3oPpDBrG8QkeAZh2QSdZD3FQ5UVy2xRmKs8/edit#gid=0).

With a few exceptions like France and Malaysia, the general pattern seems to be that they are directly or indirectly British-influenced? The big competing pattern is a Latin- and Slavic-style street + number. So, I guess I’d call this “prefix” as you suggest, maybe without the “extractor” part: `prefix_number` and `prefix_street`. That leaves room for a later `postfix_number` and `postfix_street`.

Countries matching this format:

- Australia
- France
- Hong Kong
- India
- Ireland
- Malaysia
- New Zealand
- Philippines
- Saudi Arabia
- Taiwan
- United Kingdom
- United States
- Vietnam

Each repository should configure the hooks it needs through .pre-commit-config.yaml, you *could* copy the file pre-commit creates into a directory and configure it via `core.hooksPath` as above, but you'll still need to place a file per repository (and then pre-commit will likely know less about your repository). So, in a world where we can take advantage of SSLContexts (and yes, I know that *I* am holding that up) can we not load more than one path for a certificate bundle? Isn't that what `load_verify_paths` (probably not the exact name) does? If so, could certifi accomodate that and just have a way to keep track of this?

As I understand @thenewguy's current proposal, we would open certifi's PEM file and append the "trusted" one to it. This isn't something we should do and it's certainly not something we will have permission to do.

Let's say that a system is provisioned for a user that includes certifi. If that user doesn't have sudo access and certifi was installed globally, that user will not have permissions to update the certificate bundle. So we need to accomodate that use case. And besides, even if the user does, there's nothing to stop the user from including a malicious root to allow them to MITM connections on that box. I don't think we want to enable that vector.

That said, having a way for a program to say (for it's own execution) "Add this to the list of trusted sources" makes sense to me I think, but that won't solve this use case. (Especially if you're using a library that uses requests but doesn't provide a way to specify a bundle.)

I don't think there's a great way to do this. Calling `get_ipython().kernel.do_one_iteration()` should process incoming messages, but it's likely to end up in a mess, because:

- If there are more execution requests queued, it will process those (running later cells) before the current cell finishes.
- It may change state, e.g. for redirecting output to appear under a particular cell, which will produce unexpected results when the rest of the code in that cell executes. The assumption that one cell executes at a time is built into our kernel machinery.

So I don't think it's really feasible to synchronously run Javascript on the frontend.

We encourage people to develop extension to play with what a better UI could be, and to formalize the metadata structure. 

Typically you use author singular, should it be plural ? Is it a string ? An object with first/last/given name ? A github handle ? Is there a field for primary author ?

When we designed the multiple selection, we iterated through a couple of
different models, some quite different than what we settled on. It turned
out to be one of the more difficult UX and interaction design questions we
have tackled. There were some really subtle problems that came up with

using it (I haven't seen that yet).

I'm one of the developers @chadrik mentioned, and I just wanted to add my voice in support of this, and more specifically, in favor of the `metadata` keyword.

Within development circles, the concept of "metadata" is pretty universally understood at this point, and I think the value of a keyword that is effectively self-documenting outweighs any of the potential advantages of choosing a shorter alternative.

> Programs might have uses for the container that go beyond descriptive properties. A database column, for instance, is not really metadata IMO.

The only thing "metadata" really says is that its contents have no direct impact on the functionality of the entity it is attached to. Whether those contents affect the functionality of a containing **program** is irrelevant. A database column, for instance, is absolutely metadata.

It might, but as I said, things get a bit weird. Autograd doesn't descend into data structures looking for Nodes, so when you pass around that object ndarray with Nodes inside, primitives will just call the underlying numpy functions on the array without unboxing the Nodes first. Most or all of those numpy primitives might still function properly, since they could just unpack the object array and dispatch to arithmetic operations defined on the objects contained in the ndarray, but I suspect at least some would be unhappy. Furthermore, if you returned an object ndarray like that to a function that you're differentiating with `jacobian`, I think you'd get a message that the output is independent of the input (since it looks like the output isn't itself a Node, even though it contains Nodes) and an incorrect zero value for the Jacobian.

The computation probably wouldn't be what you want anyway, since using object arrays kind of defeats the purpose of using a dense linear algebra library in the first place. The methods I suggested ensure that you're working with dense arrays of floats that are thinly boxed by autograd Nodes.

The second derivative test for `logpmf` fails when `mu` is somewhat small (0.00605613 in the Travis CI build above).

I believe this might be due to numerical instability around 0, since the analytical first derivative of `logpmf` w.r.t. `mu` is `k / mu - 1`, and the second derivative is `-k / mu**2`. Moreover, the unit test passes when a small offset is added to `mu`, e.g.:
```python
def test_poisson_logpmf(): combo_check(stats.poisson.logpmf, [1], [np.round(R(4)**2)], [R(4)**2 + 1.1])
```

I'm unsure how to fix this problem. Operating in log-space (then reversing by exponentiation) works only for `k` greater than `mu`, which is not always the case. I'll investigate this further, but any help would be much appreciated!

So the pros and cons are:

Pros of targeting dev: Get the newest features (in planemo and in tools under test - e.g. datatypes, https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/1113, https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/1153)  fastest by default, more planemo users will find Galaxy bugs prior to releases.
Pros of targeting master: Planemo will be more stable by default.

Obviously planemo can be used to target either, but ``--install_galaxy`` (now implicit) has become so fast that I think most people do not actually ever configure a Galaxy.

Sorry for not having any examples of this available. Maybe by thinking about your workflow together, we can come up with a good example and add it to the docs?

The idea is that you would run:

    # `guest` is whatever you named your module in peru.yaml
    peru override add guest ../guest-repo

Then you make your edits in `guest-repo/somefile`, and you rerun `peru sync` in your `host` project to pull them in. Rather than fetching the latest commit from`guest`'s GitHub url (or whatever), `peru` will just read whatever files are sitting at the `../guest-repo` path.

Running `peru sync` manually to pull in the changes can be a little bit annoying. However, the goal is that it should be easy to integrate `peru` into whatever script or testing command you were about to run afterwards. So for example, if you normally call `make test` in your `host` repo after you've pulled in changes, you might consider putting `peru sync` at the top of the list of commands that `make test` runs. (Some [more interesting integrations with `make`](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/tree/master/docs/make_examples) are possible too.)

@zancas 

I have looked at your new testing for client SSL, and reworked your coding, it should be backwards compatible with what you did and at the same time not break the convention we are using in the SDK. Your old solution was deviating from it and that would cause unnecessary problems. Also your usage of 'key', 'cert' the way you had is depricated, and utilizing 'certKeyChain' array is preffered, which is what the below addresses too. I cannot seem to run your test for client_ssl so can you please verify the below locally, and if all OK i will push this to my fork and we can start merging:

````````

class Client_Ssl(Resource):
    def __init__(self, client_ssls):
        super(Client_Ssl, self).__init__(client_ssls)
        self._meta_data['required_json_kind'] =\
            'tm:ltm:profile:client-ssl:client-sslstate'

    def create(self, **kwargs):
        """Allows creation of SSL profile with just key/cert names
        otherwise it will just require 'name' kwarg to be provided
        """
        if 'certname' and 'keyname' in kwargs:
            kwargs['name'] = kwargs['certname'][:-4]
            kwargs['certKeyChain'] =\
                [{'cert': kwargs['certname'], 'key': kwargs['keyname']}]

        self._create(**kwargs)
        return self

````````

For future: we don't have a policy of rebased PRs only. If you find it too troublesome, you are just free to merge with `master`. Likewise, you can add new commits with fixes on top of existing PR fixes. In fact this strategy works better with GitHub, where comments can easily get lost when rebasing. IMHO messing with git history causes too much pain to by used in any circumstances.

For anybody with this issue it is possible to work round with Apache and mod_rewrite using the following code

`RewriteEngine on`
`RewriteCond %{HTTPS} off`
`RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} !(Windows\ NT\ 5.1|Windows\ NT\ 5.2) [NC]`
`RewriteRule ^ https://%{HTTP_HOST}%{REQUEST_URI}`

Basically pop this in your :80 .conf and Windows XP users will not be redirected to https but everything else will. You can add other user agents as required.

Why not also cache pip packages?

So being completely honest, I don't know if we want this. Sure, this script combines the step of bootstrapping and running `bootstrap/dev/venv.sh` but I think that's the only real gain here. Bootstrapping is only done once and will not even be needed if they tried out the client using `letsencrypt-auto` before contributing.

One could make an argument that this helps a new contributor in the case that the entry points change and the client needs to be installed again. This is true only in the case that the developer runs `letsencrypt-dev` before running tests. If they run `tox` first, they'll get an error. Additionally, while this may be worth considering in the long term, it is not in the short term as there are very few open issues that require that this be done and none of them are tagged "1.0 for launch".

This script places all testing executables in a directory that is likely to be in a very different location than the Python files the user is editing. What's the workflow here? In my personal configuration this means I edit my files in `/home/bmw/Code/letsencrypt` and then run all my tests in `/home/bmw/.local/share/letsencrypt-dev/bin`. This seems suboptimal.

@pde, if you want to make it easier for new contributors, I'd recommend the following:
* Suggest they try the client using `letsencrypt-auto`, saying this will install all necessary dependencies. Maybe include one line pointing them to the bootstrap scripts if they'd like to do this step manually.
* Have them run `bootstrap/dev/venv.sh` to setup the development environment. This script can be moved to a different location if you'd prefer.
* Shorten the documentation about activating the `virtualenv` and not having to reinstall after making changes. We can debate the approach of using terminal specific state, but I think activating the `virtualenv` simplifies the process of running the client, `tox`, and integration tests. If we don't include this in the documentation, then we need to explain how the user goes about running the programs without activating `virtualenv`.

As a very busy LE developer, I very much appreciate trying to make it easier for new contributors to join the project, I'm just not sure if this is the right approach. If there are other benefits to the `letsencrypt-dev` approach that I'm not thinking of, please let me know.

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/cant-use-letsencrypt-auto-importerror-no-module-named--io/2345/3

This is my temporary solution:
1. install openssl
`brew install openssl
`
2. add these environment variables by running in terminal (or you can copy paste it to .bashrc and restart terminal) 
```
export ARCHFLAGS="-arch x86_64"
export LDFLAGS="-L/usr/local/opt/openssl/lib"
export CFLAGS="-I/usr/local/opt/openssl/include"
export PATH="/usr/local/opt/openssl/bin:$PATH"
```

Finally: Run

The purpose for "Django>=x.x,<x.x.99" was that I wanted to exclude beta version and release candidates, because e.g. `1.12b1 < 1.12`. I'm sure of 99.9% that new major versions will not work without many fixes and anyone would usually only waste time if he tries it or even if he tries to fix all issues if he don't want to became a regular contributor. This a special consequence of the current Django-salesforce implementation and I don't know any similar package where must be an universal "package<version" before that version is released.

The highest Django patch release version was 1.4.22 in the 1.4 LTS feature release. The number 99 is very improbable because patch versions are usually released at the beginning of month for the period of mainstream support. Special security releases are much less frequent. Django-salesforce is usually upgraded to the new Django feature release before the tenth patch. This is a reserve one order.

Another reason was a historical incompatibility of beta version parsing and compare in package managers etc. like setuptools, distribute, distutils, pip, tox... Maybe it is now better.

The main reason why I want to reject this fix is a compatibility with version version numbers in [tox.ini](https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/blob/master/tox.ini) file. We should change all or nothing.

I removed the ``location`` arg from the ``solarposition`` methods and replaced it with ``latitude``, ``longitude``, and ``altitude`` args. So, now you can use the solar position calculators without needing to construct the annoying location objects. As discussed elsewhere, the ``Location`` and ``LocalizedPVSystem`` classes will wrap ``get_solarposition``. 

A side effect (benefit?) is that the solar position calculators will now assume UTC if the input time is not localized to a timezone. No more ``location.tz`` magic.

Tests are now working for the modified clear sky and solar position signatures. I changed the clear sky testing solar position method to ``'nrel_numpy'`` and moved ``altitude`` from an arg to a kwarg. So, the tests are failing because the output has changed by a few W/m^2 and I haven't yet updated the expected output.

Should note that #70 adds a test that fails with this combination and adds a hack to the travis config file so that the tests are run on numpy 1.8 and scipy 0.14.

>I wonder if the problem is that not all file types include all metadata?

Yes.
But most scientific providers at least include information on time reference, i.e. UTC.
So with this and the coordinates, we could derive the timezine and calculate the location for the typical input meto files.

Timezone is derived either by web query to geonames or local libraries.

>For example the PVsyst output file doesn't include any timezone or lat/long information, because it just >references a .SIT file that includes that information. Not sure how to handle this other than to have the >user define a Location separately to use.

Correct. Actually, in the case of PVSyst, I would assume that a location exists because PVSyst hourly output usually does not include GHI. I would assume that a pvlib user uses this data to compare the result of both modelling environments.

So my current proposal would be address in iotools:

1. Standard output of a `pvlib.iotools.FORMAT` reader would be a tuple with 
  - dataframe with raw data (for comparison & debugging)
  -  dataframe with renamed columns to match pvlib convention
  - metadata dictionary as suggested by @wholmgren in [iotools: reader for maccrad #279](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/pull/279#discussion_r90881672)
2. `pvlib.iotools.util` to include some tools
  - optional tool to retrieve the timezone
  - optional tool to localise dataframe
  - further functions, e.g. checker if the radiation starts before sunrise to inform that there is a timezone issue

So depening on the dataset, the user coud employ the functions in `util` for more convenience.
The capabilities of each format / reader could be documented in the docstring.

>I know that this is a matter of taste but I prefer attributes instead of using the methods like functions. If one method calculates aoi than the other method should use self.aoi. I thought the new thing of oop is that the object contains inputs and outputs.

There have been a lot of angry words spewed on this topic on the internet. I used to use a lot of side effects and no return values. My code inevitably turned into a nasty mess. Of course, it's possible to write bad code using any design pattern. 

I suppose that there's something to be said for the idea that if we're going to make both frameworks then the OO framework should be as high-level as possible so that it's most clearly separated from the functional framework.

As a compromise of sorts, the [``SingleAxisTracker.get_irradiance``](https://github.com/wholmgren/pvlib-python/blob/pvsystem/pvlib/tracking.py#L106) method currently looks for a couple attributes if they're not provided as kwargs. The idea here was that the system's attributes (surface_tilt and surface_azimuth) are probably linked to the solar position, so it might make sense to assign all of these attributes to the system during your modeling. I don't know if this is a good idea or not.

It is not exactly that. As it says here:
http://dynamorio.org/docs/transparency.html
```
DynamoRIO must avoid interfering with the semantics of a program while it executes. Shifting execution from the original application code into a cache that occupies the application's own address space provides flexibility but complicates transparency. To achieve transparency, DynamoRIO cannot make any assumptions about a program's stack usage, heap usage, or any of its dependencies on the instruction set architecture or operating system. DynamoRIO's transparency restrictions necessarily apply to a client aswell. Contents:
```

In general, `ParseSMTLIB2String` does not parse anything other than the expressions in (assert ...) commands in the SMT2 file, no other commands whatsover. For instance, it also ignores all (check-sat) commands. The reason for this is that SMT2 is a command language, but while parsing the file we do not want to execute any commands. 

You can however parse arbitrary expressions (e.g., before you pass them to the optimization engine); if you provide it with existing sorts and func_decls (from the previous parts of the problem), then it will match them variables/functions up correctly (see for instance the .NET version of [ParseSMTLIBString](http://z3prover.github.io/api/html/class_microsoft_1_1_z3_1_1_context.html#a29e251ed7a6013f7abcc6d099c0ef359); the 4 arguments after the filename define the symbol table).

I can see why that would be useful, and the implementation looks good. But I'm not so keen on add this.

The main reason is the overloading of the copy methods. `copy_if_newer` may seem like small addition to the signature, but it is on equal footing with other flags that might be useful. And if we add those we end up with a  function that is harder to test and maintain.

I think it would be best to keep `copy_fs` as a "do one thing and do it well" method. Which is admittedly a departure from FS1 which tried to pack in as much functionality as possible.

I'd like to suggest either implementing a new function (e.g. `copy_fs_if_newer`), or by using a more generic mechanism. A custom Walker may be able to do this.

For instance...

```
copy_fs(foo_fs, bar_fs, walker=NewerFilesWalker(foo_fs, bar_fs))
```

Always open to other viewpoints and I appreciate your contributions. :)

Currently the state getting from `CFGNode.input_state` is generated from simulating one path reaching that node from the entry point. So clearly it's not possible to get such a state without generating `CFGAccurate`, since `CFGAccurate` is basically a numerical simulation for paths combined with forced execution of all branches at branching points.

However, you can definitely limit the state extraction to the scope of a `Path`. That is, if you know exactly which path you want to follow, you can first get such a `Path` instance in angr by providing proper input to a `Path` generated from an initial state, and then manually dump the path after each call to `step()`. This may not be feasible on complex programs like httpd or nginx since there are usually a) events, callbacks, and multi-threading or even multi-processing, and b) unimplemented function summaries (`SimProcedure`s) involved. If that's the case, you can either start your path later in the program (like, starting at the beginning of a handler instead of at the entry point of the program), or implement a minimal set of `SimProcedure`s required to follow that path. It's worth noting that in this way, it's not symbolic execution, but concrete execution (or rather, simulation), since you are supposed to give concrete input to trigger that path. There will be no force execution involved in this solution. If this is not what you want (i.e. you really want to do force execution of the path), please let me know.

Ok! This is incredibly dumb.

So this is specifically the fault of the intersection between fastpath mode and single-stepping. Fastpath mode is an extremely lightweight execution mode designed for use during CFG recovery that aims to do as little as possible. Most relevantly, it doesn't execute ccalls. You can see exactly what "fastpath" means at the bottom of angr/sim_options.py.

Since calculating the eflags at every step is expensive, VEX takes a shortcut and doesn't actually calculate/store the flags, it just stores the last operation that changed the flags along with its operands. When it gets to an instruction that uses the flags, like a branch, it calls out to a ccall that uses these condition registers to figure out what the result of the branch should be.

However! vex's optimizer certain cases so that it doesn't have to do the ccall, instead inlining the actual computation of just the flag that matters. It can only do this when the comparison and the branch are in the same basic block. Therefore, using num_inst=1 will disable vex's optimization and force ccall usage, and using fastpath will prevent this from actually working.

Basically, don't use fastpath. It's meant for static analysis, not emulation.

It depends on your goal. After reading the link you gave in your opening question it looks like your goal is to gain some knowledge about the arguments to certain functions so that you can perform some sort of run-time (or emulation-time) argument checking. If that is the case you can certainly check the arguments at each call instruction like you have just shown. If you choose to do it that way, you must enumerate and hook all instance of `call malloc`. In general that can be a difficult thing to do as it assumes a correct disassembly. Another solution would be to hook the actual malloc function, or rather, the plt entry for malloc. That is already done by angr via the malloc `SimProcedure`. So for that method you could just add code directly to [malloc](https://github.com/angr/simuvex/blob/master/simuvex/procedures/libc___so___6/malloc.py). If you go that route, you will have direct access to function arguments inside the `SimProcedure` so you don't need to instantiate your own `cc`.

The `SandboxFile` object is deprecated, so do not consider that one.

Some batch systems will setup a working directory for you, while others will not. Let me assume that your system does that on `/scratch` and that the working directory of the job is there. (If this doesn't happen, it is easy to make Ganga do this for you, just ask). 

### For scenario I:
Something like
```
j.inputfiles=[LocalFile(namePattern='input1.data', localDir='/data')]
j.outputfiles=[LocalFile(namePattern='output1.data', localDir='/data/results')]
```
In this case, before your executable starts, `/data/input1.data` will be copied to the working directory in `/scratch`. At the end of the job, the file 'output1.data' will get copied to `/data/results/output1.data`

### For scenario II:
simply define your input in the `j.inputdata` instead. In this case, nothing will be copied. Instead there will be a `magic` file in the working directory named '__GangaInputData.txt__' that has the content 

```
file:///data/input1.data
```
with one file per line. It is then for your job to read this file to understand which data should be processed.

### For scenario III:
At the moment, we do not really support this.

Sure. Just so you know, it will probably be a tricky change that will require a few iterations to get right.
Here are the steps, (can be done in different order):
- Modify the [vz-line-chart](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/tree/master/tensorboard/plugins/scalars/vz_line_chart) so that there is a setting for explicitly configuring the y scale domain
- Modify the [scalars plugin](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/master/tensorboard/plugins/scalars/scalars_plugin.py) so that it has a route for providing metadata on what the y scale domain is for a given tag. It would be nice to do this in a way where we can use it as a general pattern across plugins for retrieving per-tag metadata (we might want to include it in the index route)
- Modify the [tf-scalars-dashboard](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/tree/master/tensorboard/plugins/scalars/tf_scalar_dashboard) so that it uses this new metadata from the route to properly set the setting on individual charts
- Add a scalars() method in tensorboard/plugins/scalars/__init__ that creates a summary op using SummaryMetadata to get this new information from the api to the text plugin, kind of like [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/python/summary/text_summary.py#L46), except instead of making an empty TextPluginData, we define a new ScalarPluginData proto that has the needed fields. I would do this one last as it will be easier if we wait a bit for dust to settle on the TensorFlow and TensorBoard API side. As you all pointed out, this condition would not ever happen in current UART implementations. What I'm trying out currently is USB CDC UART driver, and I've encountered the following problem: when NCP is initialized, it tries to report it's state after reset. At this point, the USB CDC Port is still closed on the device side, hence the USB driver reports an error, which in current implementetion I forward to NCP, as I thought it should handle this situation.

If, as you say, an error is not expected in this case, I can handle the closed-port situation on the driver side, and simply buffer the message for future sending (if I'm guaranteed that mUartBuffer will not change in a meantime), but I would back @darconeous, that some assert would be appreciated, as it would simplyfy debugging in the future (it took me some to figure out why NCP is not responding). Do you find it ok to simply assert the error code for now?

The Thread Group Certification Harness is the official test harness used to test a Thread product for conformance and interoperability.  OpenThread's python scripts only implement a subset of the Thread Certification Test Plan.

After thinking about this issue for some time, here is my proposal:

1. Modifying existing [otPlatSettings*](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/settings.h)  key values MUST only be done by appending new fields.  Existing fields MUST NOT be deleted or modified in any way.
2. To support backward compatibility (rolling back to an older software version), code reading and processing key values MUST process key values that have longer length.  Additionally, newer versions MUST update/maintain values in existing key value fields.
3. To support forward compatibility (rolling forward to a newer software version), code reading and processing key values MUST process key values that have shorter length.
4. New Key IDs may be defined in the future with the understanding that such key values are not backward compatible.

Once we stabilize, the persistent settings required by Thread should be very infrequent.  Given that, I don't think we need to have a more involved versioning mechanism.

Thoughts?

This code could be problematic if OpenThread is using the task queue to "busy-wait" until some condition variable changes.  The Mac class had an example of this, but was recently removed in #706.  In general, you should make sure to process any interrupts from platform drivers - doing so will ensure that you don't get stuck when OpenThread is waiting for a state change/event in the platform drivers.

Of course, if OpenThread is not waiting on a state change from the driver, then you may be running into an actual bug that involves endless task processing.

Some other thoughts on the interface:

You could make the interface slightly nicer by making the `disable_*` and `enable_*` methods return the object, e.g.
```C++
docstring_options &enable_user_defined() { global_state().show_user_defined = true; return *this; }
```
which allows:
```C++
docstring_options doc_opts;
doc_opts.enable_user_defined().disable_signatures();
```

There's a second modification you could make to the interface that would make it less error-prone, but unfortunately can't be combined with the above.  The current code allows:
```C++
docstring_options().enable_user_defined();
```
which won't actually do anything useful.  You could prevent this by lvalue-qualifying the enable/disable methods, i.e.:
```C++
void enable_user_defined() & { global_state().show_user_defined = true; }
```
which makes
```C++
py::docstring_options().enable_user_defined();
```
a compile-time error.  Unfortunately, it also makes chaining (my first suggestion) a compile-time error.

> @wjakob said:
> One thing that does bother me a bit is the addition of a framework like Catch/GTest. Maybe we can discuss this?

There are two things that can make use of a C++ testing framework: the interpreter-related APIs and the Python C++ API (types, functions, exceptions, etc.).

The interpreter APIs pretty much must be tested within an executable. A formal C++ test framework is optional, but I think it would be worth having. The current tests within this PR are only part of what needs to be tested. Additional tests will be needed for multiple embedded modules, subinterpreters, restarting the interpreter, and some additional utility functions. This can definitely be done with just having a simple fatal error when something goes wrong, but I dislike the idea of losing detailed failure reports and introspection offered by test frameworks. It would make diagnosing issues more difficult. Without a framework, it's possible to create some assertions macros (instead of a fatal error) and logging functionality, but that's just going in the direction of reinventing a test framework.

The other thing is the Python C++ API which doesn't need to be tested in an executable/C++ tests, but it would make things easier and reduce some of the testing boilerplate of going from C++ to Python just for an assertion. E.g. the following is a simple one-liner test in Catch:
```c++
REQUIRE_THROWS_WITH(py::eval("nonsense code ..."), Catch::Contains("invalid syntax"));
```
It would otherwise require creating dedicated functions on both the C++ and Python sides. This isn't a huge deal, but it would be nice to avoid some boilerplate when writing tests.

> @jagerman 
> I'm definitely not a fan of cmake invoking a download, and am fairly sure downstream package redistributors are going to find that objectionable as well.

The default is to check if the headers are installed locally and only download if not found. I don't think any distributions will have an issue with that (CMake script download things all the time). I like the automated download because it makes it very convenient for first-time contributors to get started. Including the `catch.hpp` header is also a possibility, but I personally don't like it because that would make it a non-optional part of the repository, but it's only needed for development and not for simply using pybind11.

For complex arguments, you're going to have to handle this yourself.  We're still bound by C++ rules here: the `"s"_a = SomeClass()` is constructing to object before the `def` member function even gets invoked.  Pybind then gets this `SomeClass` rvalue, casts it into a Python object, then uses *that* Python object as the default.

If you want to avoid construction happening until it's invoked on the Python side, you'll need to workaround it.  One possible way is to take a `py::args` and/or `py::kwargs` argument then sorting out named/positional arguments and defaults yourself.  Another way is to register the function (or in this case, the constructor) twice: once with the argument *without* the default, and a second time without the argument at all: the second one would then call the first function, constructing a default argument along the way.

As suggested, the header `embedded.h` has been renamed to `embed.h` and the CMake target `pybind11::embedded` -> `pybind11::embed`. `PYBIND11_ADD_EMBEDDED_MODULE` lost the `_ADD_` and the extra parentheses. `PYBIND11_MODULE` is now available for extension modules with a matching syntax.

A full new documentation page has been added for embedding. It should cover everything from getting started to a few notes for advanced features.

As for subinterpreters and restarting, support and tests are included for all Python versions with a few caveats -- see the last two sections in the new docs page. Implementing PEP 489 would iron out the remaining issues, however, it would require a large rework of the `internals` structure but only benefit Python >= 3.5. Right now, I'm actually pretty happy with the all-Python-version solution which required only a small change to internals (changing the stored capsule from `internals *` to `internals **`) while the tradeoffs are not unreasonable: 

1. Restarting the interpreter is only possible with pybind11's functions and not with `Py_Initialize` / `Py_Finalize` (errors when re-initializing modules).

2. Restarting the interpreter leaks a bit of memory because of the `PyObject`s in `internals` which may not be garbage collected. But this happens in Python in general even without pybind11. I did some quick tests by restarting the interpreter a few million times and the leak ends up being a few megabytes -- the presence of pybind11 makes only a minimal difference.

3. Embedded modules are shared between the sub-interpreters instead of being fully independent.

Implementing PEP 489 would entail breaking up `internals` into module-specific chunks and attaching the data to the module state so that it can be ref-counted and cleaned up together with the module (instead of being global persistent data). But such a drastic change to `internals` doesn't seem worth it at this point.

Now, with PEP 489 out the window, the new module macro syntax isn't a prerequisite anymore. However, I would still advocate for the new syntax for future-proofing and dropping the module name duplication.

A macro would be reasonable, it will certainly deal with `enum_`, but it won't work with enum34 as is :/ (first, because enum34 can't be derived from `class_`, and second, it cannot give you a `class_` lvalue, only a by-value when the instance is rvalue -- to prevent defining methods on a type that may get recreated). It's possible to handle all this lvalue/rvalue mess with crtp and proper template methods, but not with macro.

Hi!

If you modify `certbot-auto` on your machine you'll need to run it with `--no-self-upgrade` to prevent it from auto-upgrading when a new release is available, and thereby overriding your modification.

The correct way to send us a PR for this would be to modify `letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto.template`.  Look at where we set up to jump from `sh` to Python:

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto.template#L331L344

You would want to add that ` -W ignore::DeprecationWarning` Python flag if and only if `$QUIET == 1`. But note that we [aren't naming the python interpreter explicitly](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto.template#L340) so you'll have to start doing that, without breaking anything on any platform that this script runs on :)

While there's certainly some benefit to logging any warnings, I'm not sure we want to do that here. The cryptography maintainer told me that we don't have to worry about them removing this functionality anytime soon and I worry that logging it creates visual noise that can confuse users thinking there is (or is about to be) a problem with their installed versions.

The goal would be making it easy to debug/write/convert large workflows in Jupyter. There are two  ways to implement this,

1. Record steps to the kernel as they are executed. Logically speaking, executing a cell with `[]` header is similar to pushing a step (instead of a variable) to the kernel, and executing a new cell might involve the execution of such cells.
   * Cons:
        * The workflow is invisible to users.
        * It is unclear what should be done to execute a `[]` step.
        * Complicated to handle re-execution of cells (replace steps with new content).

2. Scan the whole document for steps under certain conditions. For example, if `%run work` is the only line in a cell, we can scan the notebook to collect all cells with `[ ]` header and execute the workflow `work`.
    * Pros:
        * The workflow is visible.
        * There is no need to execute cells.
    * Cons:
         * Not sure how to handle cells without `[]`. It is likely that we will need to introduce a new `[global]` section to make it clear  a cell is global.

Initially, `ParamSpec` was only meant to be the very thin adapter between the python API and the database. Basically just a class for saying "there will be a column called blah with values of type bluh". But as the database became aware of more things (dependencies), the `ParamSpec` also grew and is now perhaps a good representation of a "Parameter" (as in: something we record and possibly vary, not limited to an instrument setting).

The `ManualParameter` was improved a lot with the last restructure of parameters. The name `ManualParameter` is now just a shim for a special case of `Parameter`. So perhaps they've got a bit less unwieldy? I would - like Mikhail - hope that you never have to use anything else than a `Parameter` to cover your needs.

In any case, I maintain that `ParamSpec`s should be allowed to be directly registered. Further discussion of `Parameter` improvements probably belongs in a dedicated discussion issue. I would be interested to hear use cases about `ParamSpec` versus `Parameter`.

hmm, good point. Other options I can think of are :
1. mark all new tasks pending with the task dependency manager on `SubmitTask` (so there's a tradeoff between the overhead of that and iterating over remaining placeable queue).
2. piggyback the policy, which already iterates over the queue. (This is undesirable, because it breaks the abstraction, as we'd need the policy to have access to /handle on the task dependency manager).
3. since it's a fifo queue , we can keep a pointer to the last placeable task that was registered. This could be kept as an extra private field in the SchedulingQueue class.

Depending on the policy, this queue may be large.

I'm not an HLS specification expert, but I think your HLS playlist is not valid:

**The playlist duration is to short:**

> The server MUST NOT remove a Media Segment from a Playlist file
   without an EXT-X-ENDLIST tag if that would produce a Playlist whose
   duration is less than three times the target duration.  Doing so can
   trigger playback stalls.

[Source: https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-23#page-39](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-23#page-39)

**The playlist reload time is calculated correctly:**

> When a client loads a Playlist file for the first time or reloads a
   Playlist file and finds that it has changed since the last time it
   was loaded, the client MUST wait for at least the target duration
   before attempting to reload the Playlist file again, measured from
   the last time the client began loading the Playlist file.

>  If the client reloads a Playlist file and finds that it has not
   changed then it MUST wait for a period of one-half the target
   duration before retrying.

[Source: https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-23#page-45](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-23#page-45)

Regarding env variables - in general a library approach is preferred which allows for purely programmatic usage - a stated goal of this project - and also makes it possible for developers to use env variables to generate the library's arguments if they want.

The more general library approach leaves generation open to any implementation without having to develop explicit support for the varying implementations, of which there are many.

Not sure I understand the use case. Why would a component from another model have an issue with arrays created by climt? it would anyways get the correct dimensions in `array_call`.

I think the scenario where users are setting full 3D initial conditions is more realistic than them setting a constant value, and it must be easy to do so, without having to check dimension names, specifying units etc., and just using a numpy array if that is all that is available.

Since this is already possible in the released versions, this really feels restrictive and like a step backwards.

If NetCDFMonitor is initialised with `write_on_store=True`, then it won't be able (should'nt be) to cache the initial data.

Like I said, it is really easy for a user to inspect the state output by `get_default_state` and wonder why an array that they expect to be 3D (they specified dimensions, after all) is a scalar.

Oh, ugh, there's another complication in gluing these libraries together that I forgot about: Qt allows for re-entering the main loop, like by calling `processEvents` recursively inside `processEvents`. This is kind of a gross hack that IIRC is used in a few places where they decided doing things in a proper non-blocking way was just too annoying. Trio doesn't support this. I think this is a big problem for "Qt on Trio".

> I don't see a problem in letting the caller send to the cancel scope some information about him or about the cancellation "reason" when he invokes the cancel operation on the cancel scope.

What's the use case? Why should a cancelled task need to know who triggered the cancellation or why? It might need to distinguish hard vs. soft cancel (terminate the connection vs. abort the current operation), but (a) that's a tightly-constrained value instead of an arbitrary reason, (b) often we just don't know (if a nursery cancels you because of an exception in another task, how would it know which type of cancellation to trigger?), (c) you can run the connection in a different cancel scope than the operation, thus the problem may not be *that* prevalent in the first place.

You want to distinguish different types of cancellation? you create different scopes and then decide which scope(s) to run the task under – @njsmith 's ideas already let you do that.

What's more, I don't **want** a task to be able to dynamically decide to ignore a cancellation, or what to do depending on an unconstrained tag value. It's a cancellation. You get cancelled. End of story. If you want to do something else, call it something else – i.e. add code to throw an exception into a scope. That would allow the code within the scope to recover, which a cancellation intentionally does not.

> And second, I insist in not trying to desperately simplify the overall design too soon.

I disagree. The current design is "simple". We already are complicating it by splitting off a `cancel_status` from a `cancel_scope`.

Trio works because it intentionally limits things you're able to do, which enables new concepts to emerge (cf. "Go[to] Considered Harmful"). We shouldn't add interesting things to it before we have identified a use case that requires them. I don't see a use case for tags. Even if one should emerge, we can add it later. That's better than allowing people to (ab)use a tagged cancel for things cancellation was never intended to do.

@liyanhui1228 Thanks, I see, but that's my point, if you have a probability sampler that takes priority over the trace options from the header, then you really cannot force tracing a request for the sake of debugging, instead, you would have to either make several requests so that the sampler eventually decides to sample yours or change the sampler to sample with `1.0` probability, but then of course, it will sample all other requests, not just the one you want to explicitly trace. The other aspect is service-to-service calls. The downstream service must know about the sampling decision of the upstream service and respect that so that the trace continues down the call tree. And I'm guessing the sampling decision is propagated through the trace option value from the X-Cloud-Trace-Context header.

If you look at the PHP instrumentation they follow the logic I'm proposing:

https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-php/blob/77e75c84851dd9a977924bee711bdef23110ca9f/src/Trace/RequestHandler.php#L102

If tracing is explicitly disabled from the header, then of course do not trace. Otherwise respect the trace option from the header - if that says trace, then trace, otherwise consult the sampler.

(that's because it's testing for explicit falseity, while it sets the enabled attribute to null in case the trace option is missing from the header): 
https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-php/blob/32468e5cc3e1b6135c968a414f7d043b33699872/src/Trace/Propagator/CloudTraceFormatter.php#L47

Having GEMM is also helpful for backends as well. Suppose you are backend using CuDNN. You are probably going to have something like GEMM available directly. However, if ONNX only standardizes the unfused version, then *every* backend has to implement the peephole optimization to convert the atomized representation back into the full one. It's not something that can be implemented once in ONNX, because, after all, there's no way to express the optimized operator in ONNX if you don't standardize the fused one! Conversely, we *could* implement a "decompose" operation in ONNX, to replace GEMM with simpler ONNX operators, in a way that is universal for all importers. So, for the ecosystem, it seems better to have it.

I think there is a limit to this reasoning, because I agree: we shouldn't be adding fused operators to the spec willy nilly. The argument for fusion makes the most sense when a kernel is particularly performance critical. GEMM (and convolution) seem to fall into this regime.

Basically, TL 2.0 API has been motivated by a few points:

### Being more like Keras
New Deep Learning tend to favor Keras by a large marging. Why ? It's because Keras has been designed with an intuitive API, very easy to manipulate and to use.

I won't go into much details, but I think we can agree that Keras API is a piece of good design.

### Why using TL and not Keras then ?

By trying to support different backends and to be (excessively) simple, Keras is unfortunately very slow (compared to other solutions) and incredibly difficult to use when you want to realize something not supported out of the box.

How many times we see people on stackoverflow struggling to do simple stuff with TF not managing to make it work in Keras.

### TL needs a more systematic and logical approach

I will take a simple example.

```
net_in = tl.layers.InputLayer(...)
net_1 = tl.layers.Conv2d(net_in, ...)
net_2 = tl.layers.Conv2d(net_1, ...)

print(net_2.all_params) # W and b for both Conv2d Layers => 4 params
print(net_2.all_layers)  # net_in, net_1 and net_2
print(isinstance(net_2, tl.layers.Layer))  # True
```
So we have Layer objects which behave like Network objects. Am I supposed to understand Layer objects as Network objects ? Why when you save a Layer, you actually save a Network ?

It's not consistent, not logical, not intuitive. We are used to this mindset, but it doesn't mean it's good.

### Conclusion

To summarise, what we try to achieve with TL 2.0 is simply to make TensorLayer as easier library to use on a day to day basis. 

Also to reduce the library maintainance workload, we are less than 8 people working actively on this. We are not supported by any large company (GAFAM/BATX). We do this on our free time, we need to make sure to keep it as efficient as possible.

It's true, TL 2.0 will break code compatibility. However, you can solve it in two ways:
- The code update will be very fast even for large projects (<30min-1h)
- TL 1.x will get bug fixes and will still be available if you have some code in production and have no time to update your code base. Please be sure that you pinned your dependencies and you'll be fine ;)

TL 2.0 will be a **huge** step forward to a more professional and efficient library. We are really excited about it and hope you will share our excitement ;)

In any case, if you feel like some features should be added in TensorLayer, or you always wanted that TL could do something. Please feel free to share sudo-code, we'll try our best to fit it inside the new API if it fits our roadmap ;)

@universewill I think I know what is the reason.
Nmslib and hnswlib behave a bit differently when k is very large. 
When ef<k, hnswlib overrides the main search paramter ef to k, leading to (almost) guaranteed return of total k closest neighbors and more accurate but slower search. 
Nmslib uses a different approach - it returns only the neighbors that the algorithm could find for the supplied ef value (the number of returned neighbors potentially may be much lower than k).

This seems to be your situation, look at the modified demo code:
```
from __future__ import print_function
import numpy as np
import hnswlib
import nmslib
import time


data = np.random.randn(100000, 2048).astype(np.float32)

#
print('hnswlib')
p = hnswlib.Index(space = 'l2', dim = data.shape[1])
p.init_index(max_elements=data.shape[0], ef_construction=200, M=16)
p.set_ef(20)
p.add_items(data)

tic = time.time()
labels, dists = p.knn_query(data[1, :], k=1500)
toc = time.time()
print ('h search top 1500 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels[0]))

tic = time.time()
labels, dists = p.knn_query(data[10, :], k=2000)
toc = time.time()
print ('h search top 2000 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels[0]))

tic = time.time()
labels, dists = p.knn_query(data[100, :], k=6000)
toc = time.time()
print ('h search top 6000 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels[0]))

labels, dists = p.knn_query(data[1000, :], k=8000)
toc = time.time()
print ('h search top 8000 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels[0]))

print('nmslib')
n = nmslib.init(method='hnsw', space='l2')
n.addDataPointBatch(data)
n.createIndex({'post': 0}, print_progress=True)

tic = time.time()
labels, dists = n.knnQuery(data[1, :], k=1500)
toc = time.time()
print ('n search top 1500 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels))

tic = time.time()
labels, dists = n.knnQuery(data[10, :], k=2000)
toc = time.time()
print ('n search top 2000 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels))

tic = time.time()
labels, dists = n.knnQuery(data[100, :], k=6000)
toc = time.time()
print ('n search top 6000 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels))

labels, dists = n.knnQuery(data[1000, :], k=8000)
toc = time.time()
print ('n search top 8000 time used:', toc -tic)
print('total neighbors found:',len(labels))
```

While I'm +1 on changing the default behavior here, the zope.interface issue is actually a bug in pip with how it resolves version requirements in packages.

I'll explain my version of what's going on without looking at pip's source.

    # OS X 10.6.8
    virtualenv -p python2.6 env

    env/bin/python
    >>> import pkg_resources
    >>> pkg_resources.get_distribution('zope.interface')
    zope.interface 3.5.1 (/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python)

Users are able to successfully run `env/bin/pip install "pyramid==1.2.1"`. They then want to create a project and run `env/bin/paster create -t pyramid_starter demo`. This causes the below error:

    pkg_resources.VersionConflict: (zope.interface 3.5.1 (/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python), Requirement.parse('zope.interface>=3.8.0'))

During the install they saw:

    Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): zope.interface>=3.5.1 in /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python (from pyramid==1.2.1)

Explanation:

`pyramid` pins `zope.interface>=3.5.1`, `zope.component>=3.6.0`
`zope.component` pins `zope.interface>=3.8.0`

It would appear as if pip is accepting the first "pin" it finds, and ignoring the z.c requirement of 3.8.0, causing issues later. This is not an issue with easy_install, which actually fails while doing `easy_install "pyramid==1.2.1"`:

    error: Installed distribution zope.interface 3.5.1 conflicts with requirement zope.interface>=3.8.0

In both cases it's possible to upgrade `zope.interface` within the virtualenv and then continue on, but for new users it's certainly not obvious.

My PR making the tiny changes that help tornado was accepted, so with the next tornado release, the ZMQPoller will work entirely without issue.

The only case where the pyzmq IOLoop and tornado 2.1.2 IOLoop with our ZMQPoller will behave differently is if you terminate the *context* of one or more active sockets while the IOLoop is running, the pyzmq IOLoop will catch this error and exit cleanly, while the tornado IOLoop will let the ZMQError raise.  This is probably an extremely rare case

I can draw up a couple of examples for working with tornado, but right now, you can use pyzmq with tornado with:

```python
from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop
from pyzmq.eventloop.ioloop import ZMQPoller

loop = IOLoop(ZMQPoller())
loop.install() # registers as global tornado _instance
```

I did not do it this way in the `install()` function, because the little changes to error logging just merged into tornado are really necessary for a reasonable experience if there are errors.  After the next tornado release, I may reverse the `install() / _instance` logic to create a tornado IOLoop and set it as the pyzmq instance, rather than vice versa, so we stop doing anything unofficial with tornado at all.
